title: Causal-Plasticity Atlas: Mapping Mechanism Invariance, Plasticity, and Emergence Across Heterogeneous Observational Contexts
problem_statement: Given heterogeneous multi-context observational datasets, systematically classify causal mechanisms as invariant, plastic, or emergent across contexts, producing a navigable archive of mechanism-change patterns with robustness certificates.
key_insight: By defining a proper metric on the space of context-indexed structural causal models via the Jensen-Shannon distance (JSD) between conditional mechanism distributions, and lifting this metric to a four-dimensional plasticity descriptor that decomposes mechanism change into parametric, structural, emergence, diversity, and context-sensitivity axes, we can cast the problem of multi-context causal mechanism classification as a quality-diversity archive search over a well-structured behavior space, yielding both a navigable atlas of mechanism-change patterns and formal robustness certificates for each classification. The central intellectual move is recognizing that the space of 'how a mechanism changes across contexts' is itself a rich geometric object — not a binary invariant/varying label — and that information-theoretic descriptors of this space enable both rigorous statistical guarantees and curiosity-driven exploration that discovers mechanism-change patterns a human analyst would miss.

novelty_claims.claim: Multi-Context Causal Model with variable-set mismatch and formal emergence detection
novelty_claims.what_is_new: We define the first formal framework (MCCM) that allows the variable set V_c to vary across contexts, formalizing emergence (a variable X_i exists in context c but not c') and disappearance as first-class citizens of multi-context causal analysis. The emergence relation (D12) requires not only variable absence but low mutual information I(X_i; V_{c'}) < epsilon, distinguishing truly new causal variables from recodings or renaming. No prior multi-environment causal framework handles variable-set mismatch systematically.
novelty_claims.what_is_known: ICP (Peters et al., 2016), JCI (Mooij et al., 2020), and CD-NOD (Zhang et al., 2017) all assume a fixed variable set across environments. The transportability framework (Bareinboim & Pearl, 2014) handles different populations but assumes a common causal graph structure with selection diagrams, not variable-set differences. Graph matching across variable-set mismatch has been studied in bioinformatics (network alignment) but not in the context of causal mechanism comparison.
novelty_claims.evidence: The MCCM formalization (D3) introduces a universal variable pool V_bar with V_c ⊆ V_bar varying per context, the DAG alignment operator (D6) handles partial injections between different vertex sets with explicit missing-variable penalties, and the emergence relation (D12) provides a testable information-theoretic criterion for true emergence. Together these enable classification of mechanisms that appear, disappear, or transform across contexts — a capability absent from all existing causal transfer frameworks.

novelty_claims.claim: Fine-grained plasticity spectrum with four-dimensional information-theoretic descriptors
novelty_claims.what_is_new: We replace the binary invariant/non-invariant classification of ICP with a continuous four-dimensional plasticity descriptor ψ_i = (mean_mechanism_distance, structural_plasticity_rate, emergence_rate, context_sensitivity) that decomposes mechanism change into orthogonal axes. The descriptor uses the Jensen-Shannon distance (JSD) — the square root of JS divergence — as the base mechanism distance, which is a proper metric (satisfying the triangle inequality), is always finite (unlike KL divergence), and applies to both discrete and continuous distributions without requiring absolute continuity. The plasticity spectrum (D8) then provides a formal taxonomy: invariant, parametrically plastic, structurally plastic, and emergent, with explicit threshold-based decision boundaries.
novelty_claims.what_is_known: ICP produces binary invariant/non-invariant labels. The ICM principle (Schölkopf et al., 2012) motivates mechanism-level comparison but provides no quantitative decomposition. KL divergence and Fisher-Rao distance are standard for comparing distributions. MAP-Elites (Mouret & Clune, 2015) uses behavior descriptors for archive indexing but has not been applied to causal mechanism spaces.
novelty_claims.evidence: The five descriptor components (D7) capture complementary aspects of mechanism change: ψ^(1) measures parametric variation magnitude via pairwise JSD averaging; ψ^(2) measures how often the parent set structure changes; ψ^(3) measures how often the variable is absent entirely; ψ^(4) captures the diversity of mechanism types via entropy over a clustering; ψ^(5) correlates mechanism distance with context distance. This decomposition enables the QD archive to organize mechanisms by how they change, not just whether they change, and supports downstream tipping-point detection and robustness certification.

novelty_claims.claim: Robustness certificates with formal soundness guarantees for plasticity classifications
novelty_claims.what_is_new: We provide per-mechanism robustness certificates R_i = (κ_i, δ_i, n_min, α) guaranteeing that the plasticity classification κ_i is correct with probability ≥ 1 − α, where the margin δ_i is the L-infinity distance from the estimated descriptor to the nearest classification boundary. The certificate is valid when δ_i exceeds a concentration bound ε_est(n_min, α) that we derive explicitly. No prior work in multi-context causal inference provides calibrated, per-mechanism confidence statements about mechanism stability properties.
novelty_claims.what_is_known: Stability selection (Meinshausen & Bühlmann, 2010) provides stability guarantees for variable selection. Bootstrap confidence intervals for DAG structure are standard (Friedman et al., 1999). ICP provides p-values for invariance but not margin-based certificates. Conformal prediction (Vovk et al., 2005) provides calibrated uncertainty quantification but has not been applied to causal mechanism classification.
novelty_claims.evidence: Theorem 3 (certificate soundness) proves that if δ_i > ε_est(n_min, α/2), then P(κ̂_i = κ_i*) ≥ 1 − α, where ε_est = C' · sqrt(d_max · log(|V_bar|) + log(2/α)) / n_min). Theorem 9 (structural stability) extends this to account for DAG estimation errors: if the SHD between estimated and true DAGs is at most s, the plasticity descriptor perturbation is bounded by an explicit function f(s, n_min, d_max), and classifications with margin exceeding this bound remain correct.

novelty_claims.claim: Context-aware DAG alignment with mechanism-distance-integrated cost yielding a proper pseudo-metric on heterogeneous causal structures
novelty_claims.what_is_new: We define a DAG alignment operator (D6) that simultaneously handles structural differences (edge disagreements), parametric mechanism differences (JSD between conditional distributions), and variable-set mismatch (missing-variable penalties) in a single weighted cost function. The optimal alignment cost d_DAG defines a proper pseudo-metric on the space of DAGs over subsets of a universal variable pool. Existing DAG distances (SHD, SID) assume the same variable set and ignore mechanism parameters. Graph edit distance (GED) handles structural changes but does not incorporate distributional mechanism distances. Our alignment cost is the first to unify structural, parametric, and variable-set comparison into a single metric space.
novelty_claims.what_is_known: Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) counts edge disagreements between DAGs on the same vertex set. Structural Intervention Distance (SID, Peters & Bühlmann, 2015) measures interventional disagreement. Graph edit distance (Gao et al., 2010; Bougleux et al., 2017) handles node/edge insertions and deletions with weighted costs and has known metric properties and NP-hardness results. The DAG alignment problem is NP-hard in general by reduction from subgraph isomorphism.
novelty_claims.evidence: Theorem 1 proves that d_DAG is a pseudo-metric (non-negative, symmetric, satisfies triangle inequality) when the mechanism distance JSD satisfies the triangle inequality (which it does, being the square root of JS divergence). Part (c) shows d_DAG is a full metric on DAGs modulo variable relabeling when using any proper metric as the mechanism distance component. The alignment cost extends GED by incorporating the JSD between conditional distributions as the node-substitution cost, which has no analog in the standard GED framework.

novelty_claims.claim: Curiosity-driven quality-diversity search over causal mechanism-change space with tipping-point detection
novelty_claims.what_is_new: We apply curiosity-driven QD search (MAP-Elites with a novelty + quality-improvement acquisition function) to explore the space of mechanism-change patterns, using the 4D plasticity descriptor as the behavior characterization. The search discovers diverse mechanism-change patterns — including tipping points (abrupt mechanism transitions along context paths) — that would be missed by exhaustive enumeration or random sampling. The curiosity signal (D11) drives the search toward unexplored regions of plasticity-descriptor space, and the archive organizes discovered patterns into a navigable atlas. Tipping points are formalized as context-path discontinuities in the plasticity functional (D13), with formal consistency guarantees for detection.
novelty_claims.what_is_known: MAP-Elites (Mouret & Clune, 2015) and CVT-MAP-Elites (Vassiliades et al., 2018) provide archive-based search for diverse high-quality solutions. Changepoint detection via PELT (Killick et al., 2012) identifies abrupt shifts in sequential data. The combination of QD search with causal mechanism spaces, and the use of plasticity descriptors as behavior characterizations for archive indexing, is novel to this work.
novelty_claims.evidence: Theorem 6 guarantees that archive coverage converges to 1 under an ergodicity condition on the search operator, with rate O(K · exp(−T / (K · T_mix))). Theorem 5 guarantees consistent tipping-point detection: |t̂ − t*| → 0 in probability as sample sizes and context density increase, with no false positives asymptotically in smooth regions.

assumptions.name: Per-Context Markov Condition
assumptions.statement: For each context c ∈ C_obs, the observational distribution P_c is Markov with respect to the DAG G_c: every variable X_i is conditionally independent of its non-descendants given its parents Pa_i^c. The DAG G_c is identified only up to its Markov equivalence class (CPDAG) from observational data; all parent-set-dependent definitions (D4–D8, D12–D14) are stated with respect to the estimated CPDAG, and 'parent set' is interpreted as the set of variables adjacent to X_i with a directed edge into X_i in the CPDAG (the 'definite parents').
assumptions.justification: The Markov condition is the minimal structural assumption required for any constraint-based or score-based causal discovery algorithm (PC, GES, FCI). Without it, the conditional independence structure of the data has no relationship to the causal graph, and structure learning is impossible. The CPDAG qualification is essential because observational data alone cannot in general distinguish between Markov-equivalent DAGs. By grounding all downstream definitions in CPDAGs rather than true DAGs, we avoid the circularity identified in critique F1: parent sets are defined relative to what can be identified from data, not relative to an unobservable ground truth.
assumptions.when_it_fails: If the Markov condition fails (e.g., due to deterministic relationships, selection bias, or measurement error that violates the conditional independence structure), the estimated CPDAG may not reflect any valid causal structure. Parent set estimates become meaningless, and all downstream plasticity descriptors, classifications, and certificates inherit this error. In the presence of latent confounders (violating causal sufficiency), the Markov condition holds for MAGs/PAGs rather than DAGs.
assumptions.mitigation: When causal sufficiency is suspect, replace DAG learning with MAG/PAG learning via the FCI algorithm and replace 'parent set' with 'district set' or 'possible ancestor set' throughout. The alignment and plasticity descriptor definitions generalize to PAGs with minor modifications (the alignment cost includes an additional term for bi-directed edge disagreements). The robustness certificates (D14) remain valid because they are defined in terms of descriptor estimation error, not DAG correctness. Additionally, Theorem 9 (structural stability) provides explicit perturbation bounds: if the estimated DAG has SHD ≤ s from the truth, the plasticity descriptor perturbation is bounded by f(s, n_min, d_max), so classifications with sufficient margin remain correct even under DAG estimation errors.

assumptions.name: Per-Context Faithfulness
assumptions.statement: For each context c ∈ C_obs, the distribution P_c is faithful to the DAG G_c: every conditional independence holding in P_c is entailed by the Markov condition applied to G_c. Equivalently, there are no 'accidental' conditional independences arising from specific parameter values rather than the graph structure. This can be relaxed to adjacency-faithfulness (Ramsey et al., 2006): if X_i and X_j are adjacent in G_c, then no conditioning set renders them conditionally independent.
assumptions.justification: Faithfulness (or at minimum adjacency-faithfulness) is required for constraint-based algorithms (PC, FCI) to correctly identify the CPDAG from conditional independence tests. Without faithfulness, edges may be missed (if path effects cancel) or spuriously included, leading to incorrect parent set estimates. Under faithfulness, the CPDAG is identifiable from the population distribution, and consistent estimation is possible from finite samples. The restriction to adjacency-faithfulness is important because full faithfulness can be violated even in Gaussian models when path coefficients nearly cancel (Uhler et al., 2013), whereas adjacency-faithfulness is more robust.
assumptions.when_it_fails: Near-faithfulness violations — where conditional independences nearly hold due to parameter cancellation — cause the most severe practical failures. Constraint-based methods (PC) miss or add edges when the test statistic for a near-independence is close to the significance threshold. Components ψ^(1) (mean mechanism distance) and ψ^(2) (structural plasticity rate) of the plasticity descriptor are directly affected: a missed edge means an incorrect parent set, which corrupts the mechanism P(X_i | Pa_i) and all distances computed from it. Uhler et al. (2013) showed that unfaithful distributions have positive Lebesgue measure in Gaussian models, making this a practical rather than theoretical concern.
assumptions.mitigation: Three-layer defense: (1) Use score-based methods (GES) as an alternative to PC; GES is less sensitive to near-faithfulness violations because it optimizes a global score rather than relying on individual CI tests. (2) Set the CI test significance level conservatively (α_CI = 0.01 rather than 0.05) to reduce false edge deletions. (3) Rely on Theorem 9's structural stability bounds: if the SHD from faithfulness violations is bounded by s, the plasticity descriptor perturbation is bounded, and classifications with margin δ_i > f(s, n_min, d_max) remain correct. The robustness certificates (D14) explicitly account for this by reporting the margin to the classification boundary.

assumptions.name: Independent Causal Mechanisms (ICM)
assumptions.statement: Within each context c, the mechanism P_c(X_i | Pa_i^c) is 'independent' of the marginal distribution P_c(Pa_i^c) in the sense of Schölkopf et al. (2012): knowing the marginal distribution of the parents provides no information about the conditional distribution of X_i given its parents. Formally, in an appropriate information-geometric sense, the mechanism and the cause distribution occupy orthogonal submanifolds of the statistical model. Across contexts, mechanisms may change independently: a change in P_c(X_i | Pa_i^c) from context c to c' does not necessitate or predict a change in P_c(X_j | Pa_j^c) for j ≠ i.
assumptions.justification: ICM is the foundational assumption enabling mechanism-level comparison across contexts. Without ICM, changes in the marginal distribution P(Pa_i) across contexts could masquerade as changes in the mechanism P(X_i | Pa_i), making plasticity unidentifiable from observational data. ICM allows us to attribute observed distributional changes to the correct source: a change in the marginal (due to upstream mechanism changes or different context conditions) versus a genuine change in the local mechanism. The cross-context independence of mechanism changes is what makes the plasticity descriptor well-defined: each mechanism can be classified independently along the invariant/plastic/emergent spectrum.
assumptions.when_it_fails: ICM fails when there are shared latent causes that simultaneously affect both P(Pa_i) and P(X_i | Pa_i). For example, if an unmeasured confounder U affects both a parent X_j ∈ Pa_i and the noise term in the structural equation for X_i, then the conditional P(X_i | Pa_i) depends on the distribution of U, which also determines P(Pa_i). In this case, distributional shifts across contexts may reflect changes in the latent confounder distribution rather than genuine mechanism changes, leading to spurious plasticity detection (false positives for parametric plasticity).
assumptions.mitigation: When ICM is suspect: (1) Report an upper bound on plasticity by treating all distributional change as mechanism change; this is conservative (may overstate plasticity) but never misses true plasticity. (2) Use the do-calculus or partial interventional data (if available) to test ICM: if randomizing Pa_i produces a different P(X_i | Pa_i) than the observational conditional, ICM is violated. (3) Include ICM diagnostics in the pipeline: compute the mutual information I(P(Pa_i); P(X_i | Pa_i)) across contexts and flag mechanisms where this exceeds a threshold as potentially ICM-violating. Flagged mechanisms receive a 'conditional' robustness certificate noting the ICM dependency.

assumptions.name: Finite Contexts with Sufficient Samples
assumptions.statement: The observed context set C_obs is finite with |C_obs| ≥ 3 contexts. Each context c has n_c ≥ n_min i.i.d. samples drawn from P_c, where n_min = Ω(d_max^2 · σ^2 / ε^2 · log(|V_bar| · |C_obs| / α)) for target estimation error ε, failure probability α, maximum in-degree d_max, and maximum marginal variance σ^2. The samples within each context are independent and identically distributed; samples across contexts are independent (no cross-context data leakage).
assumptions.justification: Three contexts is the minimum needed to distinguish invariant from universally plastic mechanisms: with only two contexts, a mechanism that changes between them could be invariant-with-noise or genuinely plastic, and there is no statistical basis for discrimination. The sample size lower bound n_min ensures that mechanism distances (JSD between conditional distributions) can be estimated with error ≤ ε, which is required for the separation condition in Theorem 2 (τ_inv > 2ε_est) and the margin computation in robustness certificates (D14). The i.i.d. assumption within contexts is standard for all causal discovery algorithms; the cross-context independence assumption (A4 from the formal proposal: no instantaneous feedback) ensures that the SCM for context c does not depend on observations from context c'.
assumptions.when_it_fails: With |C_obs| = 2, tipping-point detection (D13) is meaningless (no interior points on the context path), and invariance testing has no statistical power beyond a single two-sample test. With n_c < n_min, mechanism distance estimates have high variance, plasticity descriptor estimates are unreliable, and robustness certificates may have margins δ_i < ε_est, rendering them invalid. The i.i.d. assumption within contexts fails for time-series data (temporal autocorrelation), spatial data (spatial correlation), or panel data (country-year observations with cross-sectional dependence).
assumptions.mitigation: For small |C_obs|: skip tipping-point detection when |C_obs| < 3 and issue a warning. For insufficient n_c: the robustness certificate framework naturally handles this — certificates are only issued as 'valid' when δ_i > ε_est(n_min, α), so low-sample contexts produce 'marginal' or 'invalid' certificates, transparently communicating uncertainty. For non-i.i.d. data: use block bootstrap (block size calibrated to the autocorrelation length) for time-series contexts and cluster bootstrap (clusters = observational units) for panel data. The modified bootstrap affects only the certificate generation (Algorithm 5) and does not require changes to the core definitions or theorems.

assumptions.name: Sub-Gaussian or Bounded Variables
assumptions.statement: Each endogenous variable X_i under each context c is either: (a) discrete with finite support |supp(X_i)| ≤ M for a known bound M, or (b) continuous and sub-Gaussian with parameter σ_c: E[exp(t(X_i − E[X_i]))] ≤ exp(σ_c^2 t^2 / 2) for all t ∈ R. In either case, the maximum marginal variance σ^2 = max_{c,i} Var_c(X_i) is finite and known (or conservatively estimated from data).
assumptions.justification: Sub-Gaussianity (or bounded support for discrete variables) is the tail condition required for concentration inequalities that underpin the robustness certificates (D14) and sample complexity bounds (Theorem 4). Specifically, the estimation error bound ε_est(n_min, α) = C' · sqrt((d_max · log(|V_bar|) + log(2/α)) / n_min) in Theorem 3 relies on sub-Gaussian concentration of the plug-in estimator for the plasticity descriptor components. Without tail bounds, the concentration inequalities fail and the certificates provide no probabilistic guarantee. The sub-Gaussian class is broad: it includes Gaussian, bounded, and many exponential-family distributions, covering most practical applications.
assumptions.when_it_fails: Heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., Cauchy, Pareto, or data with outliers) violate sub-Gaussianity. The concentration inequalities become slower (polynomial rather than exponential tails), requiring much larger sample sizes for the same estimation accuracy. In extreme cases (distributions without finite variance), the plasticity descriptor component ψ^(1) (mean mechanism distance) may not converge at the stated rate, and the robustness certificates are miscalibrated (actual coverage below the nominal 1 − α level).
assumptions.mitigation: For heavy-tailed data: (1) Apply robust preprocessing (Winsorization, median-based centering) before computing mechanism distances. (2) Replace sub-Gaussian concentration with median-of-means concentration (Lugosi & Mendelson, 2019), which requires only finite variance (not sub-Gaussianity) at the cost of a log(1/α) factor in the sample complexity. (3) Use robust divergence estimators (e.g., Rényi divergence of order α < 1 instead of JSD) that are less sensitive to tail behavior. (4) The robustness certificates can be re-derived under the weaker median-of-means concentration, yielding valid but wider confidence intervals.

assumptions.name: Context Regularity (Piecewise Lipschitz)
assumptions.statement: The map c ↦ P_c from the context space (C, d_C) to the space of observational distributions (with the total variation or MMD topology) is piecewise Lipschitz: there exist finitely many regimes R_1, ..., R_L such that C = R_1 ∪ ... ∪ R_L, and within each regime R_l, the distribution varies smoothly: MMD(P_{c_1}, P_{c_2}) ≤ L_l · d_C(c_1, c_2) for a Lipschitz constant L_l > 0. Tipping points (D13) occur at the boundaries between regimes. The number of regimes L is finite but unknown a priori.
assumptions.justification: Context regularity is the smoothness assumption that makes tipping-point detection (D13, Theorem 5) possible. Without it, the plasticity functional Φ_i(γ, t) along a context path γ could be arbitrarily erratic — a fractal-like dependence on context would make it impossible to distinguish genuine tipping points (abrupt mechanism transitions at regime boundaries) from noise-induced fluctuations. The piecewise Lipschitz condition (rather than globally Lipschitz) is what allows tipping points to exist: the Lipschitz property holds within each regime, but the mechanism can jump discontinuously between regimes. Theorem 5's consistency guarantee relies on this separation: within-regime increments are bounded by O(L_l · d_C(c_j, c_{j+1})), while cross-regime increments are Ω(τ_tip · d_C(c_j, c_{j+1})), enabling consistent detection.
assumptions.when_it_fails: Context regularity fails when the causal mechanism varies chaotically with context (e.g., stochastic context effects with no spatial or temporal structure), when there are infinitely many regime changes (fractal regime boundaries), or when the Lipschitz constant L_l is so large that within-regime variation is indistinguishable from cross-regime jumps at the available context resolution. In practice, context regularity is most plausible when contexts have a natural ordering (temporal, spatial, dosage) and least plausible when contexts are categorical with no inherent structure.
assumptions.mitigation: For unordered or irregularly structured contexts: (1) Use the data-derived context distance (D10) to impose a pseudo-metric structure on the context space, enabling context-path construction even when no natural ordering exists. (2) Apply adaptive tipping-point detection thresholds that adjust τ_tip based on the estimated within-regime Lipschitz constant L̂_l from the data. (3) When context regularity is questionable, the tipping-point detection module issues 'tentative' rather than 'confirmed' tipping-point labels, with an uncertainty quantification based on the permutation test p-value. (4) The robustness certificates (D14) and plasticity classifications (D8) do not depend on context regularity — they depend only on A1–A5 — so the core atlas remains valid even when tipping-point detection is unreliable.

definitions.name: Structural Causal Model (SCM)
definitions.formal_statement: A structural causal model is a tuple M = (V, U, F, P_U) where V = {X_1, ..., X_d} is a finite set of endogenous (observed) variables, U = {U_1, ..., U_m} is a finite set of exogenous (latent) variables, F = {f_i : i ∈ [d]} is a set of structural equations X_i := f_i(Pa_i, U_{S_i}) where Pa_i ⊆ V \ {X_i} is the parent set and S_i ⊆ [m] indexes the relevant exogenous variables, and P_U is a joint distribution over U. The induced DAG G(M) has vertex set V and edge set {(X_j, X_i) : X_j ∈ Pa_i}. The observational distribution P_V^M is the marginal of P_{V,U} onto V, obtained by marginalizing out U. Under Assumption A1, P_V^M is Markov with respect to G(M).
definitions.intuition: An SCM specifies the data-generating process for a single context: each observed variable is a deterministic function of its causal parents and some unobserved noise. The directed acyclic graph (DAG) encodes the causal structure — who causes whom — while the structural equations encode the functional form of each causal relationship. The observational distribution is what we actually see in data; the structural equations are the latent mechanism we aim to compare across contexts.
definitions.drives_module: core/scm.py — the base data structure for all causal reasoning. Every other definition builds on SCMs by comparing, aligning, or classifying the mechanisms they contain.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The SCM is the atomic unit of the entire framework. Without it, 'mechanism' (D4), 'mechanism distance' (D5), and 'plasticity' (D7-D8) are undefined. Removing this definition would leave the framework without a formal specification of what a causal mechanism is, making all downstream constructions vacuous.

definitions.name: Context and Context Space
definitions.formal_statement: A context is an element c ∈ C drawn from a context space (C, d_C), where C is a measurable set and d_C: C × C → [0, ∞) is a pseudo-metric satisfying: (i) d_C(c, c) = 0 for all c, (ii) d_C(c, c') = d_C(c', c) for all c, c' (symmetry), and (iii) d_C(c, c'') ≤ d_C(c, c') + d_C(c', c'') for all c, c', c'' (triangle inequality). We allow d_C(c, c') = 0 for distinct c ≠ c', since different experimental conditions may produce identical causal structures. When contexts are described by feature vectors φ(c) ∈ R^p, we set d_C(c, c') = ||φ(c) − φ(c')||_2. When contexts are unstructured labels without explicit features, d_C is derived from data using D10 (data-derived context distance). The context space is always equipped with a pseudo-metric, never left as a bare set, because the context distance is load-bearing for plasticity descriptor component ψ^(5) (context sensitivity) and for tipping-point detection (D13).
definitions.intuition: A context is a 'condition' under which data was collected: a tissue type, a country, an experimental condition, a time period. The context space organizes these conditions by similarity. Two contexts that are 'close' in context space should (under Assumption A6) produce similar causal mechanisms. The pseudo-metric structure is what enables us to detect tipping points (abrupt mechanism changes between nearby contexts) and to measure context sensitivity (whether mechanism distance correlates with context distance).
definitions.drives_module: contexts/context_space.py — context representation, distance computation, and context-path construction. Also drives tipping-point detection (D13) and plasticity descriptor component ψ^(5).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Without a context distance, tipping-point detection is undefined (you cannot measure 'abruptness' without a sense of 'nearby'), context sensitivity ψ^(5) is uncomputable, and context paths (required by D13) cannot be constructed. The pseudo-metric requirement (rather than just a dissimilarity) ensures the triangle inequality, which is needed for the DAG alignment metric properties (Theorem 1) when the context-derived information contributes to the overall distance.

definitions.name: Multi-Context Causal Model (MCCM)
definitions.formal_statement: A multi-context causal model is a family {M_c}_{c ∈ C} of SCMs indexed by a context space (C, d_C), subject to the structural coherence constraint: there exists a universal variable pool V_bar such that for each context c, the endogenous variable set V_c ⊆ V_bar. The variable set V_c may vary across contexts — some variables may exist in only a subset of contexts. For each context c, G_c = G(M_c) is the induced DAG on V_c, and P_c = P_{V_c}^{M_c} is the observational distribution over V_c. In practice, we observe i.i.d. samples D_c = {x_c^(1), ..., x_c^(n_c)} from P_c for a finite subset C_obs ⊂ C of observed contexts, with potentially different sample sizes n_c and variable sets V_c. The estimated causal structure for context c is the CPDAG Ĝ_c obtained by a causal discovery algorithm (PC, GES, or FCI) applied to D_c. All downstream definitions operate on {Ĝ_c, D_c}_{c ∈ C_obs} — estimated structures and observed data — not on the true but unobservable {M_c}.
definitions.intuition: The MCCM is the 'world model' for the entire atlas. It says: there is a universe of possible variables, and each context selects a subset of these variables and connects them with a context-specific causal graph and context-specific mechanisms. The key innovation versus ICP and JCI is allowing V_c to vary — some variables simply don't exist in some contexts (a gene isn't expressed, an economic indicator isn't measured, a brain region isn't active). This is what enables emergence detection.
definitions.drives_module: core/mccm.py — the top-level data model for the atlas. Every other module operates on or queries this structure. The MCCM is the input to the entire pipeline.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The MCCM defines the problem space. Without it, there is no formal object to align, compare, or classify. The variable-set mismatch (V_c ≠ V_{c'}) is the distinguishing feature that separates this framework from ICP/JCI and enables the emergence relation (D12). The CPDAG qualification resolves the circularity critique (F1): all downstream definitions are grounded in estimable objects, not unobservable true DAGs.

definitions.name: Mechanism and Mechanism Identity
definitions.formal_statement: A mechanism for variable X_i in context c is the conditional distribution μ_i^c = P_c(X_i | Pa_i^c) induced by the structural equation f_i^c and the exogenous distribution P_{U_{S_i}}^c, where Pa_i^c denotes the definite parents of X_i in the estimated CPDAG Ĝ_c (the set of variables with a directed edge into X_i that is present in every DAG in the Markov equivalence class). Two mechanisms μ_i^c and μ_j^{c'} are identifiable as the same mechanism if: (1) Variable correspondence: there exists a bijection σ: {X_i} ∪ Pa_i^c → {X_j} ∪ Pa_j^{c'} with σ(X_i) = X_j, and (2) Distributional equivalence: under correspondence σ, the JSD between the conditional distributions is zero: JSD(P_c(X_i | Pa_i^c), P_{c'}(X_j | σ(Pa_j^{c'}))) = 0. In practice, exact zero is never tested; instead, mechanism distance (D5) is used with a threshold.
definitions.intuition: A mechanism is 'how X_i is generated from its causes.' Two mechanisms are 'the same' if they have corresponding parent variables and produce the same conditional distribution. By grounding this in CPDAG-estimated parent sets (not true DAG parent sets), we ensure the definition is operational: it refers to quantities we can actually estimate from data. The variable correspondence σ handles the case where the 'same' variable has different names or indices across contexts.
definitions.drives_module: alignment/mechanism_identity.py — mechanism matching and variable correspondence solving. This is the prerequisite for computing mechanism distances (D5) and constructing alignments (D6).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Mechanism identity defines what it means to 'compare' a mechanism across contexts. Without it, the plasticity descriptor (D7) — which averages mechanism distances across context pairs — cannot be computed, because we wouldn't know which mechanisms to compare. The CPDAG-based parent set definition resolves critique F1 and ensures the definition is identifiable from data.

definitions.name: Mechanism Distance (JSD)
definitions.formal_statement: Given two mechanisms μ_i^c and μ_j^{c'} with a variable correspondence σ (D4), the mechanism distance is defined as d_μ(μ_i^c, μ_j^{c'}; σ) = JSD(P_c(X_i | Pa_i^c), P_{c'}(X_j | σ(Pa_j^{c'}))) where JSD denotes the Jensen-Shannon distance, defined as the square root of the Jensen-Shannon divergence: JSD(P, Q) = sqrt(JS(P||Q)) = sqrt((1/2)D_KL(P || M) + (1/2)D_KL(Q || M)) with M = (P + Q)/2 the mixture distribution. For conditional distributions, we compute the expected JSD over a reference distribution on the parent values: d_μ = E_{Pa ~ P_mix}[JSD(P_c(X_i | Pa), P_{c'}(X_j | σ(Pa)))], where P_mix = (1/2)(P_c(Pa_i^c) + P_{c'}(σ(Pa_j^{c'}))) is the equal mixture of parent marginals from both contexts. When parent sets have different cardinalities under no valid σ, we set d_μ = +∞. The JSD has the following critical properties: (i) it is a proper metric on distributions, satisfying the triangle inequality; (ii) it is always finite, bounded by 0 ≤ JSD(P,Q) ≤ 1 (for base-2 logarithms), avoiding the infinities that plague KL divergence; (iii) it is well-defined for both discrete and continuous distributions without requiring absolute continuity; (iv) it is symmetric by construction.
definitions.intuition: The mechanism distance measures 'how different is the causal mechanism for X_i across two contexts?' We use the Jensen-Shannon distance because it resolves the critique-phase issues with KL divergence (which can be infinite and violates the triangle inequality) and Fisher-Rao distance (which requires parametric assumptions). JSD is always finite, always a proper metric, works for any distributions, and has a natural interpretation as the square root of the information gain from learning which context generated a sample. The mixture-marginal expectation over parent values resolves the ambiguity (critique F2) of whose parent distribution to condition on — we use the average of both.
definitions.drives_module: alignment/mechanism_distance.py — the core distance computation used by alignment (D6), plasticity descriptors (D7, component ψ^(1)), tipping-point detection (D13), and robustness certificates (D14). This is the most frequently called computational primitive in the system.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The mechanism distance is the foundation of the entire metric structure. Theorem 1 (DAG alignment metric properties) requires that d_μ satisfy the triangle inequality — JSD does. Plasticity descriptor component ψ^(1) is defined as the mean of pairwise mechanism distances. Tipping-point detection (D13) defines the plasticity functional Φ_i(γ, t) = d_μ(μ_i^{γ(0)}, μ_i^{γ(t)}). Robustness certificate margins (D14) depend on the estimation error of d_μ. Choosing a distance that is a proper metric, always finite, and distribution-free is what enables the formal guarantees to hold without caveats.

definitions.name: DAG Alignment Operator and Alignment Distance
definitions.formal_statement: Let G_c = (V_c, E_c) and G_{c'} = (V_{c'}, E_{c'}) be two CPDAGs from an MCCM. A DAG alignment is a partial injective map α: V_c ⇀ V_{c'} satisfying: (1) Consistency: if X_i, X_j ∈ dom(α) and (X_i, X_j) ∈ E_c, then either (α(X_i), α(X_j)) ∈ E_{c'} (edge preserved) or the edge is classified as structurally plastic; (2) Maximality: dom(α) is maximal among all consistency-preserving partial injections, subject to a cost bound. The alignment cost is: Cost(α; G_c, G_{c'}) = λ_struct · |ΔE(α)| + λ_mech · Σ_{X_i ∈ dom(α)} d_μ(μ_i^c, μ_{α(i)}^{c'}; α) + λ_miss · (|V_c \ dom(α)| + |V_{c'} \ im(α)|), where ΔE(α) is the set of edge disagreements under α (edges present in one CPDAG but not the other after applying the correspondence), d_μ is the JSD mechanism distance (D5), and λ_struct, λ_mech, λ_miss > 0 are user-specified weights. The DAG alignment distance is d_DAG(G_c, G_{c'}) = min_α Cost(α; G_c, G_{c'}). Theorem 1 proves d_DAG is a pseudo-metric on the space of CPDAGs over subsets of V_bar.
definitions.intuition: The alignment operator finds the 'best correspondence' between variables in two different-context causal graphs. It simultaneously accounts for three sources of difference: structural (different edges), parametric (same edges but different mechanisms), and variable-set (variables missing in one context). The cost function trades off these three sources via weights λ. The alignment distance d_DAG measures 'how different are these two causal structures overall?' It is analogous to graph edit distance but enriched with mechanism-distance information and designed for the DAG/CPDAG setting.
definitions.drives_module: alignment/dag_alignment.py — the central alignment solver, implemented as a min-cost bipartite matching problem with structural constraints. The alignment result feeds into plasticity descriptor computation (D7), tipping-point detection (D13), and the data-derived context distance (D10).
definitions.why_load_bearing: DAG alignment is the bridge between single-context causal structures and multi-context comparison. Without an alignment, there is no way to determine which mechanism in context c corresponds to which mechanism in context c', and all pairwise mechanism distances, plasticity descriptors, and tipping-point analyses are undefined. The metric properties (Theorem 1) are essential for the CVT tessellation of the QD archive (D9) — a CVT requires a metric space. The NP-hardness of optimal alignment (Theorem 8a) motivates the bounded-degree tractability result (Theorem 8b) and the greedy heuristics in the implementation.

definitions.name: Plasticity Descriptor
definitions.formal_statement: For a mechanism mu_i (targeting variable X_i) observed across contexts C_obs, the plasticity descriptor is a vector psi_i in R^4 with four components: (1) Mean mechanism distance (parametric plasticity): psi_i^(1) = (2/(|C_i|(|C_i|-1))) * sum_{c<c' in C_i} d_mu(mu_i^c, mu_i^{c'}), where C_i = {c in C_obs : X_i in V_c} and d_mu = sqrt(JSD). (2) Structural plasticity rate: psi_i^(2) = 2/(|C_i|(|C_i|-1)) * |{(c,c') : c < c', c,c' in C_i, Pa_i^c != Pa_i^{c'}}|. (3) Emergence rate: psi_i^(3) = 1 - min_c |MB_c(X_i)| / (max_c |MB_c(X_i)| + 1), where MB_c(X_i) is the Markov blanket of X_i in context c. When X_i is absent from context c, |MB_c(X_i)| = 0. (4) Context sensitivity: psi_i^(4) = rho_S(d_C(c,c'), d_mu(mu_i^c, mu_i^{c'})), the Spearman rank correlation between context distance and mechanism distance over all pairs in C_i.
definitions.intuition: The four-dimensional plasticity descriptor decomposes mechanism change into orthogonal axes: how much the conditional distribution changes (parametric), whether the parent set changes (structural), whether the variable's causal role fundamentally changes across contexts (emergence), and whether mechanism change tracks context similarity (sensitivity). Each component is directly computable from data and drives a distinct aspect of the classification and archive organization.
definitions.drives_module: descriptors/plasticity_descriptor.py — computes the 4D feature vector for each mechanism, used as the behavioral characterization in the QD archive (D9) and as the input to the plasticity classifier (D8). This is the central representation around which the atlas is organized.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The plasticity descriptor is the interface between raw causal structures and the classification/archive/certificate pipeline. Every downstream component depends on it: the plasticity spectrum (D8) applies thresholds to ψ; the QD archive (D9) uses ψ as the behavior characterization for its CVT tessellation; the robustness certificates (D14) compute margins as distances from ψ to classification boundaries; tipping-point detection (D13) operates on the mechanism distance component ψ^(1) along context paths. The 4D descriptor resolves the dimensionality mismatch identified in critiques A11 and E.S1 by providing a single canonical definition used uniformly across theory, algorithms, and evaluation.

definitions.name: Plasticity Spectrum (Invariant / Plastic / Emergent Classification)
definitions.formal_statement: Given a plasticity descriptor ψ_i ∈ R^4 and thresholds τ_inv, τ_struct > 0, the plasticity class κ_i of mechanism μ_i is determined by the following decision rules applied in priority order: (i) Emergent: κ_i = 'emergent' if ψ_i^(3) > τ_E, i.e., X_i is absent from at least one observed context. A mechanism is fully emergent if ψ_i^(3) = 1 − 1/|C_obs| (present in exactly one context) and partially emergent otherwise. (ii) Structurally plastic: κ_i = 'struct-plastic' if ψ_i^(2) > τ_struct and ψ_i^(3) = 0, i.e., the parent set changes across some context pair beyond the noise threshold, but the variable is present in all contexts. (iii) Parametrically plastic: κ_i = 'param-plastic' if ψ_i^(2) ≤ τ_struct and ψ_i^(1) ≥ τ_inv and ψ_i^(3) = 0, i.e., the parent set is stable (or nearly so) across contexts but the conditional distribution varies by more than the invariance threshold. (iv) Invariant: κ_i = 'invariant' if ψ_i^(1) < τ_inv and ψ_i^(2) ≤ τ_struct and ψ_i^(3) = 0, i.e., the mechanism is present in all contexts with the same (or nearly same) parent set and the conditional distribution varies by less than the invariance threshold. These four classes partition the set of mechanism-context configurations. The threshold τ_inv must satisfy the separation condition τ_inv > 2ε_est(n_min) from Theorem 2 to guarantee correct classification with probability ≥ 1 − α.
definitions.intuition: The plasticity spectrum replaces ICP's binary invariant/non-invariant label with a four-category taxonomy. A mechanism is invariant if it's the same everywhere; parametrically plastic if the structure is stable but the parameters vary; structurally plastic if even the causal graph changes; and emergent if the variable itself appears or disappears. The priority order ensures that emergence (the most dramatic form of change) takes precedence, followed by structural change, parametric change, and finally invariance. This ordering matches the natural severity hierarchy: it's more notable that a variable appears/disappears than that its mechanism parameters shift.
definitions.drives_module: classification/plasticity_classifier.py — the decision logic that labels each mechanism. The classifier output (κ_i for each mechanism) is the primary output of the atlas, annotated onto each archive entry.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The plasticity classification is the core deliverable of the Causal-Plasticity Atlas. It is what a domain scientist consults to understand which mechanisms are stable across contexts (and thus candidates for transfer or generalization), which vary (and thus require context-specific modeling), and which appear/disappear (revealing context-dependent causal structure). Theorem 2 guarantees correctness of this classification under the stated assumptions, and the robustness certificates (D14) quantify confidence in each classification. Without the plasticity spectrum, the system outputs only mechanism distances — useful but not interpretable without a taxonomy.

definitions.name: Mechanism Stability Class
definitions.formal_statement: A mechanism stability class is a connected component of the graph G_eps whose vertices are mechanisms and whose edges connect mu_i and mu_j whenever: (1) psi_i and psi_j belong to the same plasticity category (D8), and (2) ||psi_i - psi_j||_inf < eps for a resolution parameter eps > 0. This yields a partition S = {s_1, ..., s_m} of the mechanism space. Each class has a prototype descriptor psi_bar_s = mean({psi_i : mu_i in s}).
definitions.intuition: Groups mechanisms with similar plasticity profiles into clusters, enabling atlas-level summaries and inter-mechanism comparisons.

definitions.name: Quality-Diversity Archive (Atlas)
definitions.formal_statement: The QD archive (atlas) is a tuple A = (B, φ, q) where: (i) B = {B_1, ..., B_K} is a tessellation of the behavior space Ψ ⊆ R^4 into K cells (bins). We use a centroidal Voronoi tessellation (CVT) of Ψ with K centroids computed by Lloyd's algorithm on a sample of plasticity descriptors. The CVT ensures cells are approximately equal-volume in the regions of Ψ where descriptors concentrate. (ii) φ: E → Ψ is the behavior characterization function mapping an atlas entry e ∈ E to its plasticity descriptor ψ_e ∈ R^4 (D7). An atlas entry e = (X_i, C_e, α_e, ψ_e, κ_e, R_e, T_e) consists of: a target variable X_i, the set of contexts C_e ⊆ C_obs in which X_i participates, the alignment α_e across those contexts, the plasticity descriptor ψ_e, the plasticity classification κ_e (D8), the robustness certificate R_e (D14), and the set of detected tipping points T_e (D13). (iii) q: E → R_{≥0} is the quality function: q(e) = |C_e| · (1 + Var_{c ∈ C_e}[d_μ(μ_i^c, μ̄_i)])^{−1} · r(e), where μ̄_i is the mean mechanism distance, Var is the variance of mechanism distances to the mean, and r(e) ∈ [0,1] is the robustness score from the certificate (D14). Each cell B_k stores at most one entry: the entry with the highest quality among all entries whose behavior falls in B_k. The archive is (ε, δ)-complete if: for every mechanism μ_i with true plasticity descriptor ψ_i*, there exists an entry e with ||ψ_e − ψ_i*|| ≤ ε and r(e) ≥ δ.
definitions.intuition: The QD archive is the 'museum' of the atlas: it organizes all discovered mechanism-change patterns into display cases (cells) arranged by their plasticity fingerprint. Each cell contains the single best example of a mechanism with that particular pattern of change. The CVT tessellation ensures the cells tile the descriptor space evenly, so the archive gives uniform coverage of all mechanism-change types. The quality function prefers entries that span many contexts, have consistent mechanism distances (low variance), and have strong robustness certificates.
definitions.drives_module: archive/qd_archive.py — the central data structure of the atlas. All search (D11), retrieval, visualization, and completeness checking operate on this archive.
definitions.why_load_bearing: The QD archive is the navigable output that distinguishes the CPA from a simple per-mechanism plasticity report. Without it, the system would produce an unstructured list of mechanism classifications. The archive organizes this list into a searchable, explorable space where a domain scientist can ask 'show me all mechanisms with high structural plasticity but low parametric plasticity' by querying the corresponding region of the CVT. The (ε, δ)-completeness criterion (Theorem 6) provides a formal guarantee that the archive eventually covers all achievable plasticity patterns.

definitions.name: Data-Derived Context Distance
definitions.formal_statement: When contexts lack explicit feature descriptors φ(c), the context distance d_C is derived from data. Let P_c and P_{c'} be the observational distributions (or their kernel density estimates from samples D_c, D_{c'}) restricted to the shared variable set V_c ∩ V_{c'}. The data-derived context distance is: d_C^data(c, c') = sqrt(d_DAG(Ĝ_c, Ĝ_{c'})^2 + MMD^2(P_c|_{V_c ∩ V_{c'}}, P_{c'}|_{V_c ∩ V_{c'}})), where d_DAG is the DAG alignment distance (D6) between the estimated CPDAGs, and MMD is the maximum mean discrepancy (Gretton et al., 2012) between the marginal distributions restricted to shared variables, computed with a Gaussian kernel whose bandwidth is the median of pairwise distances (the median heuristic). The L2 combination (Euclidean norm of the two-component vector) ensures that d_C^data is a pseudo-metric whenever both d_DAG and MMD are pseudo-metrics, because the L2 norm of pseudo-metrics is a pseudo-metric (by Minkowski's inequality).
definitions.intuition: When we don't have metadata describing how contexts differ (e.g., temperature, tissue type, country GDP), we infer context similarity from the data itself. Two contexts are 'similar' if their causal graphs are similar (low alignment distance) and their distributions are similar (low MMD). This is a fallback that ensures the context space always has a pseudo-metric structure, even when contexts are just opaque labels like 'Experiment A' and 'Experiment B.'
definitions.drives_module: contexts/context_distance.py — used when context metadata is unavailable. Critical for plasticity descriptor component ψ^(5) (context sensitivity) and for constructing context paths for tipping-point detection (D13).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Without a context distance, components ψ^(5) (context sensitivity = correlation between context distance and mechanism distance) and the tipping-point framework (D13, which requires ordering contexts along a path in context space) are undefined. The data-derived distance ensures the framework is self-contained: it does not require external context metadata, broadening the applicability to settings where contexts are arbitrary experimental conditions without a natural feature representation. The pseudo-metric property is verified (not assumed) by construction via the L2 composition theorem.

definitions.name: Curiosity Signal
definitions.formal_statement: The curiosity signal for a candidate atlas entry e with behavior φ(e) = ψ is: Curiosity(e; A) = d_cell(ψ, A) + β · Δq(e, A), where: (i) d_cell(ψ, A) is the novelty component: if ψ falls in an empty cell B_k (no current occupant), then d_cell(ψ, A) = min_{k' : B_{k'} occupied} ||ψ − ψ̄_{k'}||_2, the Euclidean distance to the nearest occupied cell's centroid; if ψ falls in an occupied cell, d_cell = 0. (ii) Δq(e, A) = max(0, q(e) − q_current(B_k)) is the quality improvement component: positive if and only if the candidate entry would replace the current occupant of cell B_k with a higher-quality entry. (iii) β > 0 is the exploration-exploitation trade-off parameter. An entry is curious if Curiosity(e; A) > 0, meaning it either fills an empty cell (exploration) or improves an occupied cell (exploitation). The curiosity signal serves as the acquisition function for the QD search loop: at each iteration, candidate entries are generated by mutation operators (context subset recombination, mechanism subset selection), and only curious entries are accepted into the archive.
definitions.intuition: The curiosity signal asks: 'Is this mechanism-change pattern new (fills an empty cell) or better (improves an existing cell)?' It drives the search toward unexplored regions of plasticity-descriptor space, ensuring the archive achieves broad coverage of mechanism-change types rather than concentrating on easy-to-find patterns. The β parameter controls how much weight to give quality improvement versus novelty: high β prioritizes improving existing entries, low β prioritizes discovering new patterns.
definitions.drives_module: search/curiosity_signal.py — the acquisition function guiding the QD search loop. Called once per candidate entry to decide acceptance.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Without the curiosity signal, the QD search degenerates to random sampling followed by elitist replacement, which provides no guarantee of coverage diversity. The novelty component ensures that the search explores underrepresented regions of the behavior space, while the quality-improvement component ensures that occupied cells converge to their best possible entry. Theorem 7(c) proves that curiosity (β > 0) accelerates convergence relative to novelty-only search. The curiosity signal is what transforms a brute-force enumeration of mechanism-change patterns into a directed exploration that discovers diverse patterns efficiently.

definitions.name: Emergence Relation
definitions.formal_statement: A variable X_i is emergent in context c relative to context c' if all three conditions hold: (1) X_i ∈ V_c (the variable is observed in context c); (2) X_i ∉ V_{c'} (the variable is not observed in context c'); (3) I(X_i; V_{c'}) < ε_emerge, where I(X_i; V_{c'}) = Σ_{X_j ∈ V_{c'}} I(X_i; X_j) is the total mutual information between X_i and all variables observed in context c', computed from the joint distribution P_c restricted to {X_i} ∪ (V_c ∩ V_{c'}). Condition (3) requires that X_i carries low mutual information with the variables available in c' — it cannot be reconstructed, predicted, or approximated from V_{c'}. If condition (3) fails — i.e., I(X_i; X_j) ≥ ε_emerge for some X_j ∈ V_{c'} — then X_i is a recoding of X_j: it is informationally redundant with an existing variable, and the alignment operator (D6) maps X_i → X_j rather than treating X_i as emergent. The emergence set for context pair (c, c') is E(c, c') = {X_i : X_i is emergent in c relative to c'}. The threshold ε_emerge is set to log(2) / sqrt(n_min) as a default, scaling with the mutual information estimation precision at the available sample size.
definitions.intuition: Emergence distinguishes truly new causal variables from variables that just have different names. If a variable X_i exists in context c but not c', it could be genuinely new (a novel gene expression program, a new economic indicator) or just a renamed/recoded version of an existing variable. The mutual information condition (3) resolves this: if X_i is highly informative about some variable X_j in c', then X_i is probably just X_j under a different name, and the alignment should match them. If X_i is informationally independent of everything in c', it is genuinely novel — a truly emergent causal variable.
definitions.drives_module: alignment/emergence_detection.py — identifies genuinely new variables versus recodings during alignment. Also contributes to plasticity descriptor component ψ^(3) (emergence rate) and the 'emergent' classification in D8.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Emergence detection is the capability that distinguishes the MCCM framework from all prior multi-environment causal frameworks (ICP, JCI, CD-NOD), which assume a fixed variable set. Without it, variables that appear in some contexts but not others would either be silently ignored (losing causal information) or misaligned (corrupting mechanism distances). The mutual information condition (3) resolves critique F5 by correctly requiring low (not high) mutual information for emergence, ensuring the formal condition matches the intuitive meaning: a truly new variable is one that cannot be predicted from existing variables.

definitions.name: Tipping Point
definitions.formal_statement: Let γ: [0,1] → C be a context path — a continuous or piecewise-linear curve through context space, parameterized so that γ(0) and γ(1) are the endpoints. For a mechanism μ_i, define the plasticity functional along γ: Φ_i(γ, t) = d_μ(μ_i^{γ(0)}, μ_i^{γ(t)}), where d_μ is the JSD mechanism distance (D5). A tipping point for mechanism μ_i along path γ is a parameter value t* ∈ (0,1) such that: (1) Near-discontinuity: limsup_{h→0+} (Φ_i(γ, t* + h) − Φ_i(γ, t*)) / d_C(γ(t* + h), γ(t*)) > τ_tip, i.e., the rate of mechanism change per unit context distance exceeds threshold τ_tip. (2) Statistical significance: a permutation test (permuting context labels along the path and recomputing the plasticity functional increment) yields a p-value below α_tip for the observed increment at t*. A structural tipping point additionally requires that the CPDAG parent set Pa_i^{γ(t)} changes at t*: Pa_i^{γ(t*−ε)} ≠ Pa_i^{γ(t*+ε)} for all sufficiently small ε > 0. In the discrete setting (finite C_obs), a tipping point is detected between consecutive contexts c_j, c_{j+1} on the context path if: (Φ_i(γ, t_{j+1}) − Φ_i(γ, t_j)) / d_C(c_j, c_{j+1}) > τ_tip and the permutation p-value < α_tip.
definitions.intuition: A tipping point is a location along a 'journey through contexts' where a mechanism abruptly changes. Imagine increasing temperature gradually: at first, a gene regulatory mechanism changes smoothly, but at a critical temperature, it snaps to a completely different form. The tipping-point detection finds these critical transitions by looking for sharp spikes in the rate of mechanism change along a context path. The permutation test distinguishes real tipping points from noise-induced fluctuations.
definitions.drives_module: detection/tipping_points.py — scans context paths for abrupt mechanism changes. Results are annotated onto atlas entries as T_e (the set of detected tipping points). Used for atlas navigation (finding 'interesting' context transitions) and scientific hypothesis generation.
definitions.why_load_bearing: Tipping points are the most scientifically actionable output of the atlas: they identify specific context transitions where causal mechanisms change abruptly, guiding experimentalists to the most informative conditions for further investigation. Without tipping-point detection, the atlas can only report static plasticity descriptors — it cannot identify where on a context gradient a mechanism transitions from one regime to another. Theorem 5 guarantees detection consistency: the estimated tipping-point location converges to the true location, and smooth regions produce no false alarms asymptotically.

definitions.name: Robustness Certificate
definitions.formal_statement: A robustness certificate for a plasticity classification κ_i ∈ {invariant, param-plastic, struct-plastic, emergent} of mechanism μ_i is a tuple R_i = (κ_i, δ_i, n_min, α) where: (i) κ_i is the estimated plasticity class (D8). (ii) δ_i = min_{b ∈ ∂K} ||ψ̂_i − b||_∞ is the margin — the L-infinity distance from the estimated plasticity descriptor ψ̂_i to the nearest classification boundary ∂K in the plasticity descriptor space. The classification boundaries are the hyperplanes/surfaces defined by the threshold conditions in D8: {ψ : ψ^(1) = τ_inv}, {ψ : ψ^(2) = τ_struct}, {ψ : ψ^(3) = 0}. (iii) n_min is the minimum sample size across participating contexts. (iv) α is the target failure probability. The certificate is valid if δ_i > ε_est(n_min, α/2) where ε_est(n_min, α/2) = C' · sqrt((d_max · log(|V_bar|) + log(2/α)) / n_min) is the concentration bound on the estimation error of the plasticity descriptor. The certificate guarantees: if valid, then P(κ̂_i = κ_i*) ≥ 1 − α, where κ_i* is the true plasticity class. The robustness score r(e) ∈ [0,1] used in the quality function (D9) is r(e) = min(1, δ_i / ε_est(n_min, α/2)) — the ratio of margin to estimation uncertainty, capped at 1.
definitions.intuition: A robustness certificate is a 'guarantee card' attached to each mechanism's plasticity classification. It says: 'This mechanism is classified as [invariant/plastic/emergent], and we are at least (1−α)×100% confident this is correct, because the estimated plasticity descriptor is far enough from any classification boundary that estimation noise cannot flip the decision.' The margin δ_i quantifies how much room there is for error — a large margin means the classification is robust; a small margin means it could change with more data or slightly different analysis choices.
definitions.drives_module: certificates/robustness.py — computes and validates certificates. Each atlas entry (D9) is annotated with its certificate status: 'valid' (δ_i > ε_est, high confidence), 'marginal' (δ_i ≤ ε_est but κ_i may still be correct), or 'invalid' (δ_i ≈ 0, the classification is on a boundary and unreliable).
definitions.why_load_bearing: Robustness certificates are what elevate the CPA from a descriptive tool to a tool with formal guarantees. Without certificates, a domain scientist has no way to assess whether a mechanism's plasticity classification is reliable or an artifact of estimation noise, small sample sizes, or DAG learning errors. Theorem 3 proves certificate soundness: valid certificates guarantee correct classification with the stated probability. Theorem 9 extends this to account for DAG estimation errors. The certificates are the primary mechanism by which the system communicates uncertainty to users, and they are essential for any downstream decision-making based on the atlas (e.g., deciding whether to transfer a mechanism to a new context based on its invariance classification).

definitions.name: Conditionally Negative Definite Kernel
definitions.formal_statement: A symmetric function kappa: X x X -> R is conditionally negative definite (CND) if kappa(x,x) >= 0 for all x and for every n >= 2, x_1,...,x_n in X, and alpha_1,...,alpha_n in R with sum_i alpha_i = 0, sum_{i,j} alpha_i alpha_j kappa(x_i,x_j) <= 0. By Schoenberg's theorem, kappa is CND iff exp(-t*kappa) is positive definite for all t > 0.

definitions.name: Parametric Plasticity Map
definitions.formal_statement: In the purely parametric regime (fixed DAG G across contexts), each mechanism mu_i^c is parameterised by theta_i^c = (beta_c^{(i)}, sigma_c^{2(i)}) in Theta_i subset R^{d_max+1}. The parametric plasticity map Phi_i: Theta_i^K -> [0,1]^2 maps (theta_i^1,...,theta_i^K) to (psi_i^{(1)}, psi_i^{(4)}), where psi_i^{(1)} = C(K,2)^{-1} sum_{c<c'} sqrt(JSD(mu_i^c || mu_i^{c'})) and psi_i^{(4)} is the Spearman correlation between context and mechanism distances.

theorems.name: DAG Alignment Metric Properties
theorems.statement: The DAG alignment cost d_DAG(G_a, G_b) = min_{pi in Pi(V_a, V_b)} [ lambda_struct * |Delta E(pi)| + lambda_mech * sum_i sqrt(JSD(P_a(X_i|Pa_i^a), P_b(X_{pi(i)}|Pa_{pi(i)}^b))) + lambda_miss * |V_a Delta V_b| ] defines a pseudo-metric on the space of causal DAGs. Specifically: (i) d_DAG(G, G) = 0, (ii) d_DAG(G_a, G_b) = d_DAG(G_b, G_a) (symmetry), and (iii) d_DAG(G_a, G_c) <= d_DAG(G_a, G_b) + d_DAG(G_b, G_c) (triangle inequality). The triangle inequality holds because sqrt(JSD) is a proper metric (Endres & Schindelin, 2003), structural Hamming distance is a metric on fixed-node DAGs, and the alignment cost is a sum of metric terms optimized over a common alignment space.
theorems.proof_technique: Constructive composition of alignments. Given optimal alignments alpha: V_a -> V_b and beta: V_b -> V_c, construct the composite alignment gamma = beta circ alpha: V_a -> V_c. Show Cost(gamma) <= Cost(alpha) + Cost(beta) by decomposing each cost component: (a) structural edge differences compose sub-additively because |Delta E(gamma)| <= |Delta E(alpha)| + |Delta E(beta)| by the triangle inequality on SHD restricted to aligned subsets; (b) mechanism distances compose sub-additively because sqrt(JSD) is a proper metric satisfying triangle inequality (Endres & Schindelin 2003, Osterreicher & Vajda 2003); (c) unmatched variable penalties compose sub-additively because |V_a Delta V_c| <= |V_a Delta V_b| + |V_b Delta V_c| for symmetric differences. The key resolution from critique: use sqrt(JSD) not raw KL or symmetrized KL as the mechanism distance, since sqrt(JSD) is a true metric whereas symmetrized KL violates triangle inequality in general. This eliminates the gap identified in issue F9 regarding partial injection composition.
key_lemmas: Lemma 1.1 (Alignment Composition): For partial injections alpha: V_a -> V_b cup {bot} and beta: V_b -> V_c cup {bot}, the composite gamma = beta circ alpha is a valid partial injection V_a -> V_c cup {bot} with |dom(gamma)| >= |dom(alpha) cap dom(beta^{-1}(V_c))|.

Lemma 1.2 (Cost Sub-additivity): Under the composite alignment gamma, each cost component satisfies C_i(gamma) <= C_i(alpha) + C_i(beta) when the mechanism distance satisfies triangle inequality. The missing-variable penalty handles 'lost' variables (those in dom(alpha) but not in dom(beta)) by accounting for them exactly once in the lambda_miss term.

Lemma 1.3 (sqrt(JSD) Metric): The square root of the Jensen-Shannon divergence is a metric on probability distributions (Endres & Schindelin 2003). For multi-distribution JSD over K distributions, sqrt(JSD({P_1,...,P_K})) satisfies all metric axioms when treated as a distance from the uniform mixture.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without metric properties, the CVT tessellation of the QD archive behavior space has no geometric justification—Voronoi cells require a valid distance. The transitive alignment consistency check (Step 1.3 of the pipeline) cannot detect inconsistent alignments without the triangle inequality. The tipping-point detection cost function loses its interpretation as measuring deviation from homogeneity.
theorems.proof_sketch: Triangle inequality via alignment composition lemma: given optimal alignments alpha* (A→B) and beta* (B→C), the composite gamma = beta* ∘ alpha* is a valid partial injection, and cost sub-additivity gives d_DAG(A,C) ≤ d_DAG(A,B) + d_DAG(B,C).

theorems.name: Plasticity Classification Correctness
theorems.statement: Let M_i be a causal mechanism with true plasticity descriptor psi_i = (psi_S, psi_P, psi_E, psi_CS) in R^4. Let hat{psi}_i be the estimated descriptor from N_c >= n_min samples per context across K contexts, where n_min >= C * d_max^2 * sigma^2 / epsilon^2 * log(n * K / alpha) for universal constant C. Let tau_S, tau_P, tau_E be the classification thresholds separating invariant from plastic from emergent. If the separation condition holds—min(|psi_S - tau_S|, |psi_P - tau_P|, |psi_E - tau_E|) > 2 * epsilon—then the classification of M_i agrees with the true classification with probability >= 1 - alpha. Formally: P(ClassifyMechanism(hat{psi}_i) = ClassifyMechanism(psi_i)) >= 1 - alpha.
theorems.proof_technique: Uniform concentration inequalities on the descriptor components. For structural plasticity (JSD over parent indicators): use McDiarmid's inequality on the plug-in estimator, leveraging bounded differences when a single sample changes one parent indicator. The JSD estimator has bias O(d/N) and variance O(d^2/N^2). For parametric plasticity (average sqrt(JSD) of conditional distributions): use sub-Gaussian concentration of regression coefficient estimators combined with the Lipschitz property of sqrt(JSD) with respect to parameter perturbations. For emergence (Markov blanket ratio): use the stability of Markov blanket estimation under subsampling (Meinshausen & Bühlmann 2010). Union bound over all 4 descriptor components and all n mechanisms gives the required n_min.
key_lemmas: Lemma 2.1 (JSD Estimator Concentration): For K empirical distributions each based on N samples, the plug-in JSD estimator satisfies |hat{JSD} - JSD| <= O(sqrt(d * log(K) / N)) with probability >= 1 - delta, where d is the dimension of the support.

Lemma 2.2 (Fisher-Rao Concentration): For Gaussian linear models X = beta^T Pa + epsilon, the plug-in Fisher-Rao distance estimator satisfies |hat{d}_FR - d_FR| <= O(sqrt(d_max / N_min)) with probability >= 1 - delta.

Lemma 2.3 (Separation Sufficiency): If the separation condition tau > 2 * epsilon_est(N_min) holds for all classification boundaries, then the estimated classification is correct whenever the descriptor estimation error is bounded by epsilon_est.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without classification correctness guarantees, the entire plasticity spectrum is meaningless—users cannot trust whether a mechanism is truly invariant or merely under-sampled. The robustness certificates (T3) build on this result, and the QD archive would be populated with unreliable classifications.
theorems.proof_sketch: Each descriptor component converges to its population value: structural via Hoeffding on parent indicators (O(K^{-1/2})), parametric via regression consistency (O(N^{-1/2})), emergence via Markov-blanket stability, context sensitivity via Spearman asymptotic normality. Union bound over 4 components and n mechanisms gives the result.

theorems.name: Certificate Soundness
theorems.statement: Let C_i = (tau, beta, C) be a robustness certificate issued by Algorithm 5 (RCG) for mechanism M_i, asserting that M_i is (tau, beta)-invariant across context set C. Assume: (a) causal DAGs are correctly specified in each context, (b) samples are i.i.d. within each context with N_c >= n_min, (c) no unmeasured confounders within the measured variable set. Then: P(the true parametric plasticity Psi_P(M_i) restricted to C exceeds tau | certificate issued) <= beta. Equivalently, the certificate's claimed confidence 1 - beta is a valid frequentist coverage guarantee: over repeated sampling, at least (1 - beta) * 100% of issued certificates are correct.
theorems.proof_technique: Bootstrap consistency for the parametric plasticity estimator. The certificate is issued when the upper confidence bound UCB = Quantile(boot_max_FR_distances, 1 - beta) <= tau. Step 1: Show that the bootstrap distribution of max pairwise Fisher-Rao distances is a consistent estimator of the sampling distribution (by parametric bootstrap consistency for regression models, Efron 1979). Step 2: Show that the UCB converges to the true (1 - beta)-quantile of the max Fisher-Rao statistic at rate O(1/sqrt(N_min)). Step 3: By definition of the quantile, P(true max FR > UCB) <= beta + o(1), and for N_min sufficiently large the o(1) term is negligible. The structural invariance check (Step 1 of RCG) uses stability selection with subsample-50% x 100 rounds (per critique resolution #2), providing selection probability estimates that control the family-wise error rate at level beta via the Shah-Bühlmann (2013) bound.
key_lemmas: Lemma 3.1 (Bootstrap Consistency): The parametric bootstrap for Fisher-Rao distances between Gaussian regression models is consistent: sup_t |P*(hat{d}_FR^* <= t) - P(hat{d}_FR <= t)| -> 0 as N_min -> infinity.
Lemma 3.2 (Quantile Accuracy): The bootstrap (1-beta)-quantile hat{q}_{1-beta} satisfies |hat{q}_{1-beta} - q_{1-beta}| <= O(1/sqrt(B * N_min)) where B is the number of bootstrap replicates.

Lemma 3.3 (Stability Selection Bound): Under stability selection with subsample ratio 1/2 and B_s = 100 rounds, the probability of falsely declaring structural invariance when the true parent sets differ is bounded by q^2 / (2 * Lambda_max - 1) where q is the expected number of falsely selected variables and Lambda_max is the selection probability threshold (Shah & Bühlmann 2013).
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without certificate soundness, the formal guarantees of the CPA are vacuous. A scientist relying on a certificate to conclude that a mechanism is stable across populations would have no frequentist justification. The certificate system would reduce to an unvalidated heuristic.
theorems.proof_sketch: Three steps: (1) stability selection controls false structural invariance declarations; (2) parametric bootstrap is consistent for smooth functionals of exponential-family parameters; (3) the quantile-based UCB satisfies Pr[true > UCB] ≤ β by definition.

theorems.name: Sample Complexity for Descriptor Estimation
theorems.statement: To estimate the unified plasticity descriptor psi_i = (psi_S, psi_P, psi_E, psi_CS) in R^4 to within epsilon in L-infinity norm with probability >= 1 - alpha, it suffices to have N_min = Omega(d_max^2 * sigma^2 / epsilon^2 * log(n * K / alpha)) samples per context, where d_max is the maximum in-degree, sigma^2 is the maximum noise variance, n is the number of variables, and K is the number of contexts. This rate is minimax optimal up to logarithmic factors for the Gaussian linear SEM class.
theorems.proof_technique: Component-wise analysis followed by union bound. For psi_S (structural plasticity via sqrt(JSD)): the JSD over binary parent indicators requires N = Omega(1/epsilon^2 * log(n)) for each indicator to be correctly estimated, by Hoeffding's inequality on the indicator mean; the sqrt transformation preserves the rate up to constants. For psi_P (parametric plasticity via mean sqrt(JSD) of conditionals): estimating regression coefficients to precision epsilon requires N = Omega(d_max * sigma^2 / epsilon^2) by standard regression theory; the Lipschitz property of sqrt(JSD) w.r.t. parameters (Lipschitz constant L = O(1/sigma)) translates this to the descriptor. For psi_E (emergence via Markov blanket ratio): requires correct Markov blanket identification, which needs N = Omega(d_max * log(n) / epsilon^2) by the coherence condition of Meinshausen & Bühlmann (2006). For psi_CS (context sensitivity): inherits the rate from psi_P. Minimax lower bound via Fano's inequality on distinguishing Gaussian SEMs differing in a single edge weight by epsilon.
key_lemmas: Lemma 4.1 (Regression Coefficient Estimation): Under a Gaussian linear SEM with maximum in-degree d_max and noise variance sigma^2, the OLS estimator hat{beta} satisfies ||hat{beta} - beta||_2 <= O(sigma * sqrt(d_max / N)) with probability >= 1 - delta.

Lemma 4.2 (sqrt(JSD) Lipschitz Property): For two Gaussian conditional distributions parameterized by (beta_a, sigma_a^2) and (beta_b, sigma_b^2), the sqrt(JSD) distance is Lipschitz in the parameters with constant L = O(1/min(sigma_a, sigma_b)).

Lemma 4.3 (Minimax Lower Bound): No estimator can achieve L-infinity error epsilon for the plasticity descriptor with fewer than Omega(d_max / epsilon^2 * log(n / alpha)) samples per context, by Fano's inequality applied to a carefully constructed family of SEMs.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without sample complexity bounds, practitioners have no guidance on how much data is needed for reliable plasticity classification. The separation condition in T2 cannot be evaluated without knowing epsilon_est as a function of N_min.
theorems.proof_sketch: Upper bound: analyse each component separately—structural requires O(eps^{-2} log n) per indicator (Hoeffding), parametric requires O(d_max sigma^2 / eps^2) (regression theory), emergence inherits the Markov-blanket stability rate, context sensitivity (Spearman rho) inherits the parametric rate. Take max over components, union bound over n mechanisms and K contexts. Lower bound: Fano's inequality on distinguishing two Gaussian SEMs differing by eps in one edge weight.

theorems.name: Tipping-Point Detection Consistency
theorems.statement: Let {G_k, theta_k}_{k=1}^K be a sequence of causal models indexed by an ordered context parameter theta_k = k/K, satisfying the piecewise Lipschitz condition (B3) with finitely many true tipping points T* = {tau_1*, ..., tau_m*}. Let hat{T} be the set of tipping points detected by Algorithm 4 (TPD) using PELT with penalty lambda = C * log(K) on the mechanism divergence sequence {d_k}_{k=1}^{K-1} where d_k = sqrt(JSD(mechanisms at context k, mechanisms at context k+1)). Then: (i) Consistency: As K -> infinity and N_min -> infinity, P(|hat{T}| = |T*|) -> 1 and max_{tau* in T*} min_{hat{tau} in hat{T}} |hat{tau} - tau*| -> 0. (ii) No false positives: For any segment [s, t] containing no true tipping point, the probability of detecting a spurious changepoint within [s, t] is bounded by alpha (controlled by the permutation validation step). (iii) Localization rate: |hat{tau} - tau*| = O_p(1/K) for each true tipping point tau*.
theorems.proof_technique: Two-part argument. Part (i): PELT with BIC-type penalty is consistent for the number and locations of changepoints in piecewise-constant signals (Killick et al. 2012). The mechanism divergence sequence d_k is piecewise-smooth (by B3) with jumps at true tipping points. Under sufficient samples N_min, the estimation noise in d_k is O(1/sqrt(N_min)), which vanishes relative to the jump size Delta_tau (bounded below by the minimum mechanism change at a tipping point). The PELT cost function (within-segment variance) detects jumps when the signal-to-noise ratio Delta_tau / sigma_est -> infinity, which occurs as both K and N_min grow. Part (ii): The permutation validation test at each detected changepoint has exact Type I error alpha by construction (exchangeability under the null). Benjamini-Hochberg correction across multiple changepoints controls the FDR at level alpha. Part (iii): The localization rate follows from the convergence of the CUSUM statistic to the true changepoint location at rate 1/K for discrete signals. Per critique resolution #8: PELT operates on sqrt(JSD) mechanism divergence sequences with permutation validation, ensuring both detection power and false positive control.
key_lemmas: Lemma 5.1 (PELT Consistency): The PELT algorithm with penalty lambda = C * log(K) consistently estimates the number of changepoints in a piecewise-constant signal observed with sub-Gaussian noise, provided the minimum segment length grows as omega(log K).
Lemma 5.2 (Mechanism Divergence Estimation): The plug-in estimator of sqrt(JSD(mechanisms at k, mechanisms at k+1)) converges to the population value at rate O(sqrt(d_max * log(n) / N_min)).

Lemma 5.3 (Permutation Test Validity): Under the null hypothesis of no changepoint (exchangeability of mechanism divergences within a segment), the permutation p-value has exact Type I error control at any level alpha, for any finite sample size and any number of permutations B >= 1/alpha - 1.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without tipping-point consistency, the system might report tipping points that are artifacts of finite sampling or miss genuine regime changes. The tipping-point layer of the atlas would be unreliable, and the certificates on either side of a tipping point could not be trusted.
