theorems.proof_sketch: PELT with BIC penalty is consistent for piecewise-constant signals (Killick et al., 2012). The mechanism-distance sequence is piecewise-Lipschitz by assumption, so PELT detects all changepoints with probability → 1 as K → ∞.

theorems.name: Atlas Completeness Under Ergodic Search
theorems.statement: Let A_T be the QD archive after T iterations of Algorithm 3 (CD-QD) with CVT tessellation into L cells. Assume the mutation operator is ergodic over the genome space G = 2^{[K]} x 2^{[n]} x Phi with minimum transition probability p_min > 0 for any single-step reachable genome. Then: (i) Coverage convergence: E[|{cells occupied in A_T}|] / L -> 1 as T -> infinity for all reachable cells. (ii) Rate: The expected number of iterations to achieve (1-epsilon)-coverage of reachable cells is O(L_r * log(L_r / epsilon) / p_min) where L_r <= L is the number of reachable cells. (iii) Quality monotonicity: For each cell c, the quality q(A_T[c]) is non-decreasing in T.
theorems.proof_technique: Coupon collector argument with non-uniform hitting probabilities. (iii) is immediate from the MAP-Elites elitist replacement rule (a cell's occupant is only replaced by a strictly higher-quality genome). For (i): ergodicity of the mutation operator guarantees that every reachable genome is visited infinitely often with probability 1 (irreducibility + positive recurrence on the finite genome space). Each visit to a genome g maps to a cell c = CVT_cell(behavior(g)), which is either empty (and gets filled) or occupied (and potentially updated). Since the genome-to-cell mapping is many-to-one, each cell is visited infinitely often. For (ii): the curiosity bonus (inverse visit count) ensures that under-visited cells receive exploration priority. By the UCB analogy (Auer et al. 2002), the curiosity-weighted selection achieves regret O(sqrt(L * T * log T)), translating to coverage rate O(L * log L / T). Per critique resolution #7: CVT tessellation (not fixed grid) is used, computed via Lloyd's algorithm with sqrt(JSD) distance in descriptor space.
key_lemmas: Lemma 6.1 (Ergodicity of Mutation Operator): The mutation operator defined in Algorithm 3 (add/remove/swap context or mechanism, perturb parameters) makes the Markov chain on genomes irreducible over the reachable genome space. Any genome g' reachable from g requires at most |C symmetric_diff C'| + |M symmetric_diff M'| mutations, each occurring with probability >= 1/(4 * max(K, n)).

Lemma 6.2 (CVT Tessellation Quality): Lloyd's algorithm on a sample of S plasticity descriptors from the initial random population converges to a CVT that minimizes the quantization error E[||psi - centroid(cell(psi))||^2]. With S = 10000 samples and L = 1000 cells, the average cell diameter is O(1/L^{1/4}) in 4D space.

Lemma 6.3 (Curiosity-Accelerated Coverage): Under curiosity-driven selection with novelty weight proportional to 1/(1 + visit_count), the expected time to first visit each reachable cell is reduced by a factor of at least min(2, 1 + mu * bar{q}) compared to uniform random selection, where mu is the surprise weight and bar{q} is the average quality in the archive.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without completeness guarantees, the atlas might systematically miss certain types of mechanism-change patterns, presenting a biased view of the plasticity landscape. Users would not know whether an empty region of the archive represents a genuinely impossible pattern or merely an unexplored one.
theorems.proof_sketch: Ergodic mutation kernel assigns positive probability to every genome. By the coupon-collector argument, all reachable CVT cells are eventually occupied with probability 1.

theorems.name: Coverage Rate Bound
theorems.formal_statement: Under the conditions of Theorem T6 (Atlas Completeness), with curiosity weight mu > 0 and CVT cell count L, the expected fraction of reachable cells occupied after T iterations satisfies: E[|A_T|/L_r] >= 1 - L_r * exp(-T * p_min / (2 * L_r)). In particular, (1-epsilon)-coverage is achieved for T >= 2 * L_r * p_min^{-1} * log(L_r / epsilon).
theorems.proof_sketch: For each reachable cell k, the visitation probability per iteration is at least p_min/(2*L_r) (the factor of 2 accounts for worst-case curiosity-weighted selection dilution). The first-visit time T_k is stochastically dominated by a Geometric(p_min/(2*L_r)) random variable. Summing Pr[T_k > T] over all L_r reachable cells and applying the geometric tail bound yields the stated coverage rate.
theorems.implications: Provides an explicit convergence rate for the QD search, enabling budget allocation: given a target coverage fraction, one can compute the required number of iterations.

theorems.name: Hardness and Tractability of DAG Alignment
theorems.statement: (a) NP-hardness: Computing the optimal DAG alignment d_DAG(G_a, G_b) when no anchors are provided is NP-hard, by reduction from subgraph isomorphism. (b) Fixed-parameter tractability: When the maximum in-degree is bounded by a constant k and the number of unanchored variables is bounded by u, the optimal alignment can be computed in time O(n^2 * d^2 + u^3) where d = max in-degree. For the target regime (k <= 5, u <= 10), this is O(n^2). (c) Approximation: The greedy Markov-blanket-pruning + Hungarian matching algorithm (Algorithm 1, CADA) computes a (1 + epsilon)-approximate alignment in O(n^2 * d^2 + u^3) time, where epsilon depends on the fraction of candidate pairs pruned by the Markov blanket overlap threshold.
theorems.proof_technique: (a) Reduction from subgraph isomorphism: given two graphs H and G, construct DAGs D_H and D_G by orienting all edges according to a topological ordering of the vertices. Set lambda_mech = 0 and lambda_miss = infinity (require perfect matching). Then d_DAG(D_H, D_G) = 0 iff H is a subgraph of G under some node mapping, which is NP-complete (Cook 1971). (b) The key insight is that bounded in-degree limits the Markov blanket size to O(d^2) (parents + children + co-parents), reducing the candidate set for each unanchored variable from n to O(d^2). With u unanchored variables, each having O(d^2) candidates, the Hungarian algorithm runs on a u x u matrix with at most u * d^2 nonzero entries. Total: n iterations for anchor propagation + u * d^2 candidates for CI scoring + u^3 for Hungarian = O(n^2 d^2 + u^3). (c) The approximation guarantee follows from the fact that Markov blanket overlap pruning (threshold 0.3) removes candidates whose alignment score is guaranteed to be below 0.5 (because low MB overlap implies low CI-fingerprint agreement), so no near-optimal candidate is pruned unless its quality is substantially below the optimum.
key_lemmas: Lemma 7.1 (Markov Blanket Size Bound): In a DAG with maximum in-degree k, each variable's Markov blanket has size at most k + n_children + sum_{j in children} (|Pa(j)| - 1) = O(k^2 + k * n_children). Under bounded degree, this is O(k^2).

Lemma 7.2 (Candidate Pruning Soundness): If two variables u_a and u_b have Markov blanket overlap < 0.3 (under the anchored mapping), then the CI-fingerprint agreement score is < 0.5 with probability >= 1 - alpha, provided the tests have power >= 0.8.

Lemma 7.3 (Hungarian Optimality): The Hungarian algorithm finds the minimum-cost perfect matching in a bipartite graph in O(u^3) time (Kuhn 1955). For the alignment problem, this gives the optimal assignment among the unpruned candidates.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without the hardness result, one might attempt exact alignment algorithms on unanchored high-dimensional problems, wasting compute. Without the tractability result, the alignment step would be the computational bottleneck, making the pipeline infeasible for n >= 50.
theorems.proof_sketch: (a) Reduction from subgraph isomorphism. (b) Bounded in-degree limits candidate pairs; Hungarian matching on the pruned set costs O(u³). (c) MB pruning soundness: under faithfulness, optimal pairs have Jaccard overlap ≥ ρ > 0, so pruning at threshold ≤ ρ preserves optimality.

theorems.name: Structural Stability Under DAG Estimation Errors
theorems.statement: Let hat{G}_c be an estimated DAG differing from the true DAG G_c by SHD(hat{G}_c, G_c) <= s edges (counting additions, deletions, and reversals) in each context c. Let hat{psi}_i be the plasticity descriptor computed from the estimated DAGs {hat{G}_c} and let psi_i be the true descriptor from {G_c}. Then: ||hat{psi}_i - psi_i||_inf <= f(s, n, d_max) where f(s, n, d_max) = O(s * d_max / n + s / K) is a perturbation bound that degrades gracefully with the number of misestimated edges s. Specifically: (i) |hat{psi}_S - psi_S| <= 2s / (K * n) (each misestimated edge affects at most 2 parent indicators across at most 1 context); (ii) |hat{psi}_P - psi_P| <= O(s * d_max * sigma / N_min^{1/2}) (misestimated parents change the conditioning set, perturbing Fisher-Rao distances); (iii) |hat{psi}_E - psi_E| <= 2s / (max_c |MB_c| + 1) (each misestimated edge changes Markov blanket size by at most 1 in one context).
theorems.proof_technique: Component-wise sensitivity analysis. For psi_S: the JSD over parent indicators changes by at most O(s/(K*n)) when s parent indicators are flipped across K contexts, because JSD is Lipschitz in the empirical distribution with constant 1/K. For psi_P: a misestimated parent changes the conditioning set, which perturbs the regression coefficients by O(sigma * sqrt(d_max / N_min)) via the omitted variable bias formula. The Fisher-Rao distance inherits this perturbation via its Lipschitz property (Lemma 4.2). For psi_E: each edge error changes one Markov blanket size by at most 1, perturbing the min/max ratio by at most 1/(max MB + 1). The L-infinity bound is the maximum of the three component bounds. Per critique resolution #5: this theorem is now connected to a dedicated SHD perturbation experiment that injects controlled errors (s in {1, 2, 5, 10}) and verifies the bound empirically.
key_lemmas: Lemma 8.1 (JSD Lipschitz in Distributions): For binary distributions on {0,1}^n, changing s entries in one of K input distributions perturbs the JSD by at most O(s / (K * n) * log(K)).

Lemma 8.2 (Omitted Variable Bias): Adding or removing one parent from a linear regression changes the coefficient estimates by at most ||Sigma_{included,omitted}||_2 * |beta_omitted| / lambda_min(Sigma_{included}), which is O(sigma * sqrt(d_max / N_min)) under the faithfulness assumption.
Lemma 8.3 (Markov Blanket Perturbation): Adding or deleting one edge in a DAG changes the Markov blanket of at most 3 variables (the endpoints and one co-parent), each by exactly 1 member.
theorems.what_fails_without_it: Without structural stability guarantees, the entire pipeline is fragile to DAG estimation errors—the most common source of error in practice. This is the most practically important theorem because real causal discovery algorithms always produce imperfect DAGs. Reviewers identified the absence of this result from the algorithm and evaluation as the #1 vulnerability.
theorems.proof_sketch: Component-wise Lipschitz analysis: each misestimated edge changes ≤ 2 parent indicators (structural) or shifts regression coefficients by O(σ√(d_max/N)) (parametric). Triangle inequality for JSD transfers these perturbations to descriptor error.

theorems.name: Mechanism Distance Kernel
theorems.statement: The JSD function kappa(P,Q) = JSD(P||Q) is conditionally negative definite on the space of probability measures. Consequently: (a) the Gaussian kernel k_h(P,Q) = exp(-JSD(P||Q)/h^2) is positive definite for all h > 0; (b) for conditional mechanisms, d_mu^2 = E_Z[JSD(mu(·|Z)||mu'(·|Z))] is CND on the space of conditional mechanisms.
theorems.proof_strategy: Use the Endres-Schindelin decomposition and the integral representation JSD(P||Q) = int_0^1 (1/(t(1-t))) D_t(P||Q) dt where D_t is related to Renyi divergence. For each fixed t, the integrand is CND. Since CND functions form a convex cone closed under non-negative integration, the integral is CND. Part (b) follows because E_Z[kappa_Z] is CND when each kappa_Z is CND.
theorems.implications: Enables kernel methods (kernel PCA, kernel k-means, MMD tests) directly in mechanism space for non-parametric hypothesis testing of mechanism identity.

theorems.name: Plasticity Estimator Convergence Rate
theorems.statement: Under Assumptions A1-A5 in the Gaussian linear SEM setting, the empirical plasticity descriptor psi_hat_n satisfies E[||psi_hat_n - psi*||_inf] <= C_1 * d_max * sigma * sqrt(log(|V_bar|K)) / sqrt(n) + 1/(K-1), where C_1 = 8*sqrt(2) is an explicit constant. The first term is estimation error (vanishing with n), the second is discretisation bias (vanishing with K).
theorems.proof_strategy: Bound each descriptor component separately. For psi^{(1)}: use delta method + sub-Gaussian concentration of OLS estimators + Lipschitz property of Gaussian JSD. For psi^{(2)},psi^{(3)}: use skeleton recovery guarantees of PC/GES under faithfulness. For psi^{(4)}: use stability of rank correlations. Take maximum over components.
theorems.implications: Establishes the fundamental convergence rate for plasticity estimation, enabling finite-sample guarantees for classification.

theorems.name: Minimax Lower Bound for Plasticity Classification
theorems.statement: For the class P(d_max, sigma, K) of Gaussian linear MCCMs, any estimator kappa_hat of the plasticity class has inf_{kappa_hat} sup_{P in P} Pr[kappa_hat != kappa*] >= 1/4 whenever N <= c * d_max^2 * sigma^2 / Delta_min^2 * (log 2 - 1/K), where Delta_min is the classification margin. This matches the upper bound in Theorem T3 up to logarithmic factors, establishing minimax optimality.
theorems.proof_strategy: Le Cam's two-point method with corrected construction: perturb all d_max parent coefficients simultaneously by delta*1_{d_max}, giving KL = N*K*d_max*delta^2/(4*sigma^2). Substituting delta^2 = Theta(sigma^2*Delta_min/d_max^2) yields the d_max^2 dependence matching the upper bound. Fano extension with 2^{K/2} hypotheses (varying the perturbed context subset) tightens the constant.
theorems.implications: Proves that Theorem T3's sample complexity is optimal — no algorithm can classify plasticity with fewer samples, up to log factors.

theorems.name: Fisher-Rao Pullback on Parametric Plasticity Space
theorems.statement: In the Gaussian linear SEM setting with fixed DAG G, K >= 3 contexts, and bounded residual variances: (a) the parametric plasticity map Phi_i is smooth away from JSD=0; (b) the Fisher information metric g^F on Theta_i^K induces a well-defined pullback Riemannian metric g^Psi on the image via the infimum-pullback (quotient metric) construction; (c) for K=2, the pullback reduces to the Fisher-Rao metric on the Gaussian family.
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (a): Gaussian JSD is real-analytic in (beta, sigma^2). Part (b): positive definiteness from the fact that nonzero d(psi^{(1)}) requires nonzero d(theta) in Fisher metric (positive definite on Gaussian family). Part (c): by symmetry, minimum-norm lift sets dot_theta_1 = -dot_theta_2, reducing to standard Fisher-Rao.
theorems.implications: Shows the plasticity descriptor inherits natural geometric structure from mechanism distributions. Geodesics correspond to 'cheapest' mechanism interpolations in information-theoretic terms.

theorems.name: Uniform Certificate Validity
theorems.statement: For m robustness certificates issued by Algorithm RCG, applying the Holm-Bonferroni step-down procedure controls the family-wise error rate (FWER): Pr[exists i: certificate R_i valid but kappa_i != kappa_i*] <= alpha. The power guarantee for mechanisms with margin delta >= delta_min is at least 1 - m*exp(-n_min*delta_min^2/(2*sigma^4*d_max^2)).
theorems.proof_strategy: Holm-Bonferroni controls FWER for arbitrary dependence. Verify super-uniformity of bootstrap p-values under the null via bootstrap CLT. Power from Hoeffding's inequality on bootstrap descriptor mean. Union bound over m mechanisms for expected certificate yield.
theorems.implications: Extends per-mechanism certificate soundness to simultaneous validity over all mechanisms, essential for atlas-wide reliability.

theorems.name: Concentration of DAG Alignment Distance
theorems.statement: The alignment distance d_DAG(G_hat_a, G_hat_b) between estimated DAGs concentrates: (a) |E[d_DAG(G_hat_a,G_hat_b)] - d_DAG(G_a*,G_b*)| <= L_align * (E[SHD(G_hat_a,G_a*)] + E[SHD(G_hat_b,G_b*)]), where L_align = max(lambda_s, lambda_m*d_max*sigma^2, lambda_v)/S; (b) Pr[|d_DAG - E[d_DAG]| >= t] <= 2*exp(-n*t^2/(2*L_align^2*S)).
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (a): Lipschitz property of d_DAG from Theorem T9 + triangle inequality. Part (b): McDiarmid's inequality with bounded differences c_j = L_align * d_max / n per sample.
theorems.implications: Provides finite-sample control on alignment quality, enabling confidence intervals on mechanism-distance estimates.

theorems.name: IPU Correctness and Complexity
theorems.statement: Under the frozen-history condition (previous DAGs and alignments unchanged): (a) IPU output is identical to batch PDC on K+1 contexts; (b) IPU runs in O(K*(p*d_max^2 + u^3 + p*d_max*n)) time vs O(K^2*(...)) for batch — a factor-K improvement; (c) under DAG re-estimation, IPU differs from batch by at most O(K*L_align*E[SHD]).
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (a): algebraic identity — C(K+1,2)-pair average = weighted combination of C(K,2)-pair average and K new pairs. Part (b): count CADA calls (K vs K^2). Part (c): Theorem T9 structural stability bound.
theorems.implications: Enables online/streaming atlas updates as new contexts arrive, reducing computational cost from quadratic to linear in the number of contexts.

theorems.name: ACT Certificate Quality
theorems.statement: Algorithm ACT satisfies: (a) FWER control at level alpha via Holm-Bonferroni (validity independent of budget allocation); (b) savings of m_c*(B_total/m - B_0) bootstrap samples compared to uniform allocation, where m_c is the number of easily certified mechanisms; (c) uncertain mechanisms receive at least B_total/m bootstrap samples when at least gamma-fraction are easy.
theorems.proof_strategy: Part (a): Holm-Bonferroni validity depends on p-value super-uniformity, not sample size. Part (b): accounting — easy mechanisms use only pilot budget B_0. Part (c): redistribution inequality B_rem/m_u >= B_total/m when m_u <= m(1-gamma).
theorems.implications: Adaptive budget allocation concentrates computational effort on uncertain mechanisms while maintaining FWER guarantees.

algorithms.name: Context-Aware DAG Alignment (CADA)
algorithms.input: Two causal DAGs G_a = (V_a, E_a) and G_b = (V_b, E_b) with associated sufficient statistics (Sigma_a, Sigma_b), a set of known anchor correspondences {(v_a^i, v_b^i)}, significance level alpha (default 0.05), and maximum unanchored variable count (default 10).
algorithms.output: Alignment mapping pi: V_a -> V_b union {bot}, edge partition (S_shared, S_modified, S_context_a, S_context_b), alignment quality score in [0,1], and structural divergence descriptor delta.
key_steps: Phase 1 — Anchor Propagation: Initialize alignment pi from provided anchors. Identify unanchored variable sets U_a and U_b. If |U_a| or |U_b| exceeds max_unanchored, raise TooManyUnanchoredError.

Phase 2 — Markov Blanket Candidate Generation: For each unanchored variable u_a in U_a, compute its Markov blanket (parents + children + co-parents). For each candidate u_b in U_b, compute anchored Markov blanket overlap via Jaccard index. Retain candidates with overlap >= 0.3.

Phase 3 — Statistical Compatibility Scoring: For each (u_a, u_b) candidate pair, compute CI-fingerprint similarity (partial correlation tests at anchored neighbors, weight 0.6) and local distribution shape similarity (KL-based, weight 0.4). Build a score matrix.

Phase 4 — Optimal Matching via Hungarian Algorithm: Pad score matrix to square with -infinity for impossible assignments. Run Hungarian algorithm to find maximum-weight matching. Accept matches with quality >= 0.5; assign bot to low-quality matches.

Phase 5 — Edge Classification: For each edge in E_a, classify as shared (present in E_b under alignment), modified (reversed, deleted, or added in E_b), or context-specific (endpoint maps to bot). Symmetrically for E_b.

Phase 6 — Score and Divergence Computation: Alignment score = |S_shared| / |E_a union aligned(E_b)| (Jaccard over edges). Structural divergence delta uses weighted SHD (reversals weight 0.5, additions/deletions weight 1.0, context-specific weight 0.8), normalized by n*(n-1)/2.
algorithms.complexity_time: O(n^2 * d^2 + u^3) where n = max(|V_a|, |V_b|), d = max in-degree, u = max(|U_a|, |U_b|). With bounded degree d <= 5 and u <= 10, effectively O(n^2). For n=100: ~250K operations, runs in milliseconds.
algorithms.complexity_space: O(n^2) for the score matrix and DAG adjacency matrices.
algorithms.data_requirements: Covariance matrices (or sufficient statistics) per context. Anchor correspondences for at least 50% of variables (for practical tractability). Sample sizes N_c >= 200 for reliable CI tests.
algorithms.what_is_novel: Three-phase candidate pruning pipeline (semantic anchoring -> Markov blanket filtering -> CI fingerprinting) that reduces arbitrary DAG matching from NP-hard to polynomial. Weighted structural divergence distinguishing reversals from additions/deletions. Statistical CI-fingerprint scoring integrating observational data into structural alignment.
algorithms.what_is_known: Hungarian algorithm for bipartite matching (Kuhn 1955). Markov blanket comparison for variable identification (Huang et al. 2020). Graph edit distance framework (Gao et al. 2010). Subgraph isomorphism NP-completeness (Cook 1971).
algorithms.correctness_argument: Soundness: every alignment found by CADA is a valid partial injection (guaranteed by the Hungarian algorithm on a bipartite graph). Completeness: the Markov blanket pruning only discards candidates with overlap < 0.3, which Lemma 7.2 shows cannot be near-optimal matches under the faithfulness assumption. Optimality: among unpruned candidates, the Hungarian algorithm finds the globally optimal assignment. The overall alignment is (1+epsilon)-approximate where epsilon depends on the fraction of valid candidates pruned (Theorem T7c).

algorithms.name: Unified Plasticity Descriptor Computation (PDC)
algorithms.input: List of K aligned DAGs {G_1,...,G_K} (same variable indexing post-alignment), list of K parameterizations {Theta_1,...,Theta_K} (regression coefficients + residual variances), list of K sufficient statistics {Sigma_1,...,Sigma_K} (covariance matrices + sample sizes), significance level alpha (default 0.05).
algorithms.output: For each variable i in {1,...,n}: a unified 4D plasticity descriptor psi_i = (psi_S, psi_P, psi_E, psi_CS) with confidence intervals, and a classification label in {invariant, parametric_plastic, structural_plastic, emergent, mixed}.
key_steps: Step 1 — Structural Plasticity (psi_S): For each variable i, compute K binary parent indicator vectors B_c^i in {0,1}^n. Compute multi-distribution JSD over {B_1^i,...,B_K^i}. Apply sqrt to obtain psi_S = sqrt(JSD) which is a proper metric (critique resolution #1).

Step 2 — Parametric Plasticity (psi_P): Group contexts by parent set. Within each parent-set group, compute pairwise sqrt(JSD) between conditional distributions P_a(X_i|Pa_i) and P_b(X_i|Pa_i). For Gaussian models, use closed-form involving regression coefficients and residual variances. Average over all within-group pairs.

Step 3 — Emergence (psi_E): Compute Markov blanket sizes {|MB_c(i)|}_{c=1}^K. Set psi_E = 1 - min_c |MB_c(i)| / (max_c |MB_c(i)| + 1). High psi_E indicates variable is causally isolated in some contexts but connected in others.

Step 4 — Context Sensitivity (psi_CS): Compute the coefficient of variation of psi_P across context subsets of size ceil(K/2). High psi_CS indicates that plasticity is concentrated in specific context subsets rather than uniform.

Step 5 — Confidence Intervals via Stability Selection: Use stability selection (subsample 50% x 100 rounds, per critique resolution #2) instead of full bootstrap for structural components. Use parametric bootstrap (B=200 replicates) for parametric components. Compute (1-alpha) CIs for each descriptor component.

Step 6 — Classification: Apply threshold-based decision rules using CI lower bounds. Thresholds tau_S=0.1, tau_P=0.5, tau_E=0.5 (calibrated by permutation testing on null data). Hierarchy: emergent > mixed (structural AND parametric) > structural_plastic > parametric_plastic > invariant.
algorithms.complexity_time: O(B * n * K^2 * d_max) where B=200 bootstrap replicates, n=variables, K=contexts, d_max=max in-degree. Plus O(100 * n * K * d_max) for stability selection. For n=100, K=50, d=5, B=200: ~5*10^7 operations, runs in seconds.
algorithms.complexity_space: O(B * n * K * d_max) for bootstrap samples. O(n * K) for parent indicators. For the target regime: ~100 MB.
algorithms.data_requirements: N_c >= 500 samples per context for reliable parent set estimation (per Theorem T4). N_c >= 200 for parametric plasticity detection. At least K >= 3 contexts.
algorithms.what_is_novel: Unified 4D plasticity descriptor resolving the 4D (unified) conflict. Use of sqrt(JSD) as mechanism distance ensuring metric properties (critique resolution #1). Stability selection for structural certificates instead of full DAG re-estimation (critique resolution #2), reducing cost by ~100x.
algorithms.what_is_known: Jensen-Shannon divergence (Lin 1991). Fisher-Rao distance for Gaussian families (Atkinson & Mitchell 1981). Stability selection (Meinshausen & Bühlmann 2010). Bootstrap confidence intervals (Efron 1979). Markov blanket estimation (Tsamardinos et al. 2003).
algorithms.correctness_argument: Descriptor consistency: each component converges to its population value at the rate specified in Theorem T4, with the confidence intervals providing finite-sample coverage guarantees (Theorem T3). Classification correctness follows from Theorem T2 when the separation condition holds. The unified descriptor captures structural change (psi_S), parametric change (psi_P), variable-level qualitative change (psi_E), and heterogeneity of change (psi_CS), covering all mechanism-change modes identified in the formal framework.

algorithms.name: Curiosity-Driven Quality-Diversity Search (CD-QD)
algorithms.input: Pre-computed aligned DAGs, plasticity descriptors, alignment cache. Configuration: max_iterations (default 5000), population_size (default 100), CVT cell count L (default 1000), curiosity weight mu (default 0.5).
algorithms.output: QD archive A mapping CVT cells to (genome, behavior_descriptor, quality) triples. Each genome g = (C, M, phi) specifies a context subset C, mechanism subset M, and analysis parameters phi. Archive coverage statistics and canonical pattern catalog.
key_steps: Step 1 — CVT Tessellation: Generate S=10000 random genomes, compute their behavior descriptors in R^4, and run Lloyd's algorithm (k-means variant) to partition descriptor space into L cells with centroids. Use sqrt(JSD)-compatible Euclidean distance in descriptor space (critique resolution #7, CVT not fixed grid).
Step 2 — Population Initialization: Generate pop_size diverse random genomes. Each genome samples 2-10 contexts uniformly and 1-20 mechanisms uniformly, with random analysis parameters.

Step 3 — Iterative Search Loop (max_iter iterations): (a) Evaluate each genome: restrict pre-computed descriptors to genome's context and mechanism subsets, compute 4D behavior descriptor (fraction invariant, fraction parametric_plastic, fraction structural/emergent, normalized entropy of plasticity magnitudes), compute quality q = Shannon entropy of classifications + 0.5 * statistical significance. (b) Update archive: insert into CVT cell if empty or if quality exceeds current occupant. Update visit counts and quality EMA. (c) Compute curiosity: novelty = 1/(1 + visit_count(cell)), surprise = |q - quality_EMA(cell)|. Total curiosity = novelty + mu * surprise. (d) Select parents weighted by curiosity, generate children via mutation.

Step 4 — Mutation Operators: With probability 0.3, add/remove a context. With probability 0.3, add/remove a mechanism. With probability 0.2, swap a context. With probability 0.2, perturb analysis parameters (threshold, stringency) via Gaussian noise.

Step 5 — Post-Processing: Cluster occupied archive cells by behavior descriptor similarity. Extract canonical patterns (cluster centroids). Rank by quality and coverage contribution. Generate human-readable pattern descriptions.
algorithms.complexity_time: O(max_iter * pop_size * n * K) per search + O(S * L * d_desc) for CVT construction. With defaults: 5000 * 100 * 100 * 50 = 2.5 * 10^9 simple operations (~30-60 seconds on modern CPU). CVT: 10000 * 1000 * 4 = 4*10^7 (~1 second).
algorithms.complexity_space: O(L * (|C_max| + |M_max| + d_desc)) for the archive + O(L) for visit counts and quality EMA. With L=1000: ~1 MB.
algorithms.data_requirements: Pre-computed plasticity descriptors from ALG2. Pre-computed pairwise alignments from ALG1. No additional raw data needed beyond what ALG1-2 consume.
algorithms.what_is_novel: Application of QD search to causal mechanism space—first use of MAP-Elites for exploring mechanism-change patterns. Curiosity signal based on inverse visit count + quality prediction error, implemented without neural networks. CVT tessellation adapted to plasticity descriptor space with sqrt(JSD)-compatible distance.
algorithms.what_is_known: MAP-Elites quality-diversity algorithm (Mouret & Clune 2015). Novelty search (Lehman & Stanley 2011). Count-based exploration bonuses (Bellemare et al. 2016). CVT tessellation via Lloyd's algorithm (Du et al. 1999). Curiosity-driven exploration concept (Pathak et al. 2017).
algorithms.correctness_argument: Archive monotonicity: quality within each cell is non-decreasing by the elitist replacement rule. Coverage convergence: ergodicity of the mutation operator (Lemma 6.1) guarantees all reachable genomes are visited infinitely often, and the many-to-one genome-to-cell mapping ensures all reachable cells are filled (Theorem T6). Curiosity acceleration: the inverse visit count biases exploration toward empty cells, reducing the coverage time by at least a constant factor over uniform random search (Lemma 6.3). The CVT tessellation ensures cells have approximately equal volume in descriptor space, preventing the archive from over-representing dense regions.

algorithms.name: Tipping-Point Detection via PELT (TPD)
algorithms.input: Ordered sequence of K aligned DAGs {G_1,...,G_K} with parameterizations {Theta_1,...,Theta_K} and sufficient statistics {Sigma_1,...,Sigma_K}. Context ordering (permutation of {1,...,K}). PELT penalty (default log(K)). Minimum segment length (default 2). Significance level alpha for permutation validation (default 0.05).
algorithms.output: List of validated tipping points, each with: context location tau, p-value from permutation test, effect size (Cohen's d), list of affected mechanisms with change type (structural/parametric), and segment descriptions on each side.
key_steps: Step 1 — Mechanism Divergence Sequence: Compute pairwise mechanism divergence D[a][b] = sqrt(JSD(mechanisms_a, mechanisms_b)) for all ordered context pairs, combining structural divergence (from alignment) and parametric divergence (average sqrt(JSD) over shared edges) with equal weight 0.5 each (critique resolution #1 and #8: sqrt(JSD) as proper metric for mechanism distance).

Step 2 — PELT Dynamic Programming: Apply Pruned Exact Linear Time algorithm on the divergence sequence. Cost function C(s,t) = sum of within-segment consecutive divergences + variance penalty * segment_length. Penalty = C * log(K) (BIC-type). Pruning rule: remove candidate changepoint s if F[s] + C(s+1,t) > F[t] for current endpoint t, as s can never be optimal for any future endpoint.
Step 3 — Backtracking: Trace optimal segmentation from end to start, collecting changepoint locations.

Step 4 — Permutation Validation (critique resolution #8): For each detected changepoint tau, extract left and right segments (min_segment contexts each). Compute observed divergence between segment means. Generate 999 permutation null samples by randomly reassigning contexts to left/right segments. Compute permutation p-value = (1 + count(null >= observed)) / 1000. Apply Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction across all detected changepoints.

Step 5 — Mechanism-Level Attribution: For each validated tipping point, identify which specific mechanisms change most (comparing parent sets and regression coefficients across the boundary). Classify each affected mechanism's change as structural or parametric.
Step 6 — Effect Size Computation: Cohen's d = (observed_divergence - mean(null_divergences)) / std(null_divergences) for each validated tipping point.
algorithms.complexity_time: O(K^2 * n * d_max) for divergence matrix + O(K) expected for PELT (O(K^2) worst case) + O(B * |changepoints| * n * d_max) for permutation tests. For K=50, n=100, d=5, B=999: ~2.6*10^7 operations, under 1 second.
algorithms.complexity_space: O(K^2) for divergence matrix + O(K) for PELT arrays + O(B * n) for permutation null distributions.
algorithms.data_requirements: Ordered contexts (temporal, dose-response, or gradient). At least K >= 4 contexts (min_segment=2 on each side of a changepoint). Pre-computed alignments and sufficient statistics.
algorithms.what_is_novel: Custom PELT cost function for causal mechanism homogeneity combining structural and parametric divergence. Permutation validation providing exact Type I error control for mechanism changepoints. Mechanism-level attribution identifying which specific causal relationships undergo tipping-point transitions. Integration of sqrt(JSD) mechanism distance with changepoint detection.
algorithms.what_is_known: PELT algorithm (Killick et al. 2012). Permutation tests for changepoint validation (Antoch & Huskova 2001). BIC penalty for model selection (Schwarz 1978). Benjamini-Hochberg FDR control (Benjamini & Hochberg 1995). Cohen's d effect size measure.
algorithms.correctness_argument: Detection consistency: PELT with BIC penalty consistently estimates the number and locations of changepoints in piecewise-constant signals (Theorem T5). The mechanism divergence sequence is piecewise-smooth under assumption B3, with jumps at true tipping points. False positive control: the permutation test has exact Type I error alpha by the exchangeability argument (Theorem T5, part ii). FDR correction across multiple changepoints controls the overall false discovery rate. Localization: the estimated changepoint location converges to the true location at rate O(1/K) (Theorem T5, part iii).

algorithms.name: Robustness Certificate Generation (RCG)
algorithms.input: Aligned DAGs for context set C, parameterizations, sufficient statistics, variable index i, parametric tolerance tau, confidence level beta (e.g., 0.05 for 95% confidence). Stability selection parameters: subsample_ratio=0.5, n_rounds=100 (critique resolution #2).
algorithms.output: Certificate object containing: type (STRONG_INVARIANCE | PARAMETRIC_STABILITY | STRUCTURAL_STABILITY | CANNOT_ISSUE), validity flag, max sqrt(JSD) distance observed, upper confidence bound on max sqrt(JSD), robustness margin (tau - UCB), stability selection probabilities for parent set membership, explicit list of assumptions and validity conditions, minimum sample size warning.
key_steps: Step 1 — Structural Invariance via Stability Selection: Compute parent sets across all contexts in C. If parent sets differ, run stability selection (critique resolution #2): subsample 50% of data in each context, re-estimate DAG, record parent set for variable i. Repeat 100 rounds. Compute selection probability for each potential parent: Pi_j = (number of rounds where j is a parent) / 100. Declare structural invariance if all selection probabilities are consistently above 0.6 or below 0.4 across all contexts (i.e., the same parents are stably selected). This replaces the full bootstrap (500 DAG re-estimations per pair), reducing to ~100*K total re-estimations instead of ~500*K^2.

Step 2 — Parametric Stability Check: For contexts sharing structurally invariant parent sets, compute pairwise sqrt(JSD) distances between conditional distributions. For Gaussian models, use closed-form involving regression coefficients and residual variances.

Step 3 — Bootstrap Upper Confidence Bound: Parametric bootstrap (B=1000 replicates): perturb regression coefficients by their standard errors, perturb residual variance via chi-squared resampling. Compute max sqrt(JSD) across all context pairs for each bootstrap replicate. UCB = (1-beta)-quantile of bootstrap max sqrt(JSD) distribution.

Step 4 — Certificate Decision: If UCB <= tau, issue certificate (STRONG_INVARIANCE if UCB < 0.01, else PARAMETRIC_STABILITY). Compute robustness margin = tau - UCB. If UCB > tau, report CANNOT_ISSUE with detailed diagnostics.

Step 5 — Metadata and Validity Conditions: Record all assumptions (causal sufficiency, i.i.d. sampling, no unmeasured confounders, Gaussian conditional assumption). Flag minimum sample size warnings (N_c < 200). Record stability selection probabilities for transparency.
algorithms.complexity_time: Structural check: O(100 * K * T_DAG) where T_DAG = O(n^2 * log(n)) for GES. This is ~100K DAG re-estimations total (critique resolution #2), not 500*K^2. Parametric check: O(K^2 * B_param * d_max) with B_param=1000. For n=100, K=10: structural ~100*10*10^5 = 10^8 (minutes); parametric ~45*1000*5 = 225K (negligible). Total per mechanism: minutes, not days.
algorithms.complexity_space: O(100 * n * d_max) for stability selection results + O(B_param * K^2) for bootstrap distributions.
algorithms.data_requirements: N_c >= 200 samples per context minimum. N_c >= 500 recommended for reliable structural stability assessment. At least 3 contexts in the certificate's context set C.
algorithms.what_is_novel: First formal certificate framework for mechanism invariance across multiple causal contexts. Stability selection for structural certificates (replacing expensive full bootstrap), providing controlled error rates via the Shah-Bühlmann bound. Explicit validity conditions and robustness margins enabling users to assess certificate reliability.
algorithms.what_is_known: Stability selection (Meinshausen & Bühlmann 2010, Shah & Bühlmann 2013). Parametric bootstrap for regression models (Efron 1979). Bootstrap confidence intervals (Efron & Tibshirani 1993). Fisher-Rao and JSD distances for Gaussian families.
algorithms.correctness_argument: Certificate soundness: Theorem T3 guarantees that the claimed confidence 1-beta is a valid frequentist coverage guarantee, provided the assumptions hold. Stability selection error control: the Shah-Bühlmann (2013) bound ensures that the probability of falsely declaring structural invariance is bounded. Parametric bootstrap consistency: the bootstrap distribution of max sqrt(JSD) converges to the true sampling distribution (Lemma 3.1), making the UCB asymptotically exact. The stability selection approach (critique resolution #2) reduces computational cost by ~100x while maintaining statistical validity.

algorithms.name: Incremental Plasticity Update (IPU)
algorithms.input: Existing atlas with K contexts (DAGs, alignments, distances, descriptors). New context c_{K+1} with data D_{K+1}.
algorithms.output: Updated descriptors {psi_i[K+1]} for all variables, updated alignment set with K new alignments.
steps: 1. Estimate DAG G_hat_{K+1} from D_{K+1} using GES/PC
2. For each c in [K]: compute alignment pi_{c,K+1} via CADA
3. For each variable i and context c in [K]: compute d_mu(mu_i^c, mu_i^{K+1})
4. Update descriptors using incremental formulas: psi^{(1)}[K+1] = (C(K,2)*psi^{(1)}[K] + sum_{c=1}^K sqrt(d_mu^2)) / C(K+1,2)
algorithms.complexity: O(K * (p * d_max^2 + u^3 + p * d_max * n)), a factor-K improvement over batch PDC's O(K^2 * ...).

algorithms.name: Adaptive Certificate Tightening (ACT)
algorithms.input: Estimated descriptors {psi_hat_i}_{i=1}^m, total bootstrap budget B_total, FWER level alpha, pilot fraction gamma=0.2, boundary tolerance eta > 0.
algorithms.output: Robustness certificates with FWER guarantee. Mechanisms classified as certified, uncertifiable, or uncertain.
steps: 1. Pilot: allocate B_0 = floor(gamma*B_total/m) bootstrap samples uniformly
2. Classify: C_cert (margin > 3*SE), C_uncert (eta < margin <= 3*SE), C_boundary (margin <= eta)
3. Issue certificates for C_cert directly; mark C_boundary as uncertifiable
4. Adaptively allocate B_rem to C_uncert: w_i = SE_i^2 / max(delta_i, eta)^2, B_i = floor(B_rem * w_i / sum_w)
5. Run B_i additional bootstraps for each uncertain mechanism; recompute SE and delta
6. Apply Holm-Bonferroni across all issued certificates
algorithms.complexity: Same total bootstrap cost B_total as uniform RCG, but concentrated on uncertain mechanisms.

complexity_analysis.overall_pipeline: Three-phase pipeline. Phase 1 (Foundation): O(K * T_DAG + K^2 * n^2 * d^2 + B * n * K^2 * d) for causal discovery + pairwise alignment + descriptor computation. Phase 2 (Exploration): O(max_iter * pop_size * n * K) for QD search. Phase 3 (Validation): O(K^2 * n * d + B_perm * |cps| * n * d) for tipping points + O(n_inv * 100 * K * T_DAG + n_inv * K^2 * B_param * d) for certificates, where n_inv is the number of invariant mechanisms. Total: dominated by Phase 1 (discovery + alignment) and Phase 3 (certificates) for large n.
complexity_analysis.bottleneck: The computational bottleneck is Phase 1 causal discovery (external, O(K * T_DAG)) for the initial run, and Phase 3 stability selection (O(n_inv * 100 * K * T_DAG)) for certificates. With stability selection (critique resolution #2), the certificate bottleneck is reduced from ~500*K^2 to ~100*K DAG re-estimations per mechanism. For n=100, K=10 with ~60 invariant mechanisms: ~60 * 100 * 10 * T_DAG = 60,000 * T_DAG. With T_DAG ~ 1 second for GES on n=100: ~17 hours sequential, ~2 hours with 8-core parallelism. For n=50, K=10: ~4 hours sequential, ~30 minutes parallel.
complexity_analysis.practical_regime: The system is designed for n in [10, 100] variables and K in [3, 50] contexts. Practical sweet spot: n in [15, 50], K in [5, 20], N_c >= 500 samples per context. In this regime, the full pipeline completes in 30-120 minutes on a single CPU core (Intel i7 or equivalent). The QD search (30-60 seconds) and tipping-point detection (<1 second) are negligible compared to discovery and certificates.
complexity_analysis.scalability_limits: Hard limits: n > 200 makes pairwise alignment cache O(K^2 * n^2) memory-intensive (~1 GB for K=20, n=200). K > 100 makes the alignment cache growth quadratic (~5000 pairs). n > 500 requires variable screening (retain only variables with edges in >= 1 context). K > 200 requires hierarchical context clustering. The QD archive with L=1000 CVT cells scales gracefully in n and K. The certificate generation is the binding constraint for large n, even with stability selection.

ipu_update.description: Incremental Plasticity Update for streaming contexts
ipu_update.cost: O(K * (p * d_max^2 + u^3 + p * d_max * n))
ipu_update.improvement: Factor-K improvement over batch PDC O(K^2 * ...)

implementation_mapping.description: Multi-Context Causal Model (MCCM) data structures. SCM representation (DAG adjacency matrix, regression coefficients, residual variances) per context. Context metadata (index, features, ordering). Variable metadata (names, semantic anchors, presence/absence per context).

Mechanism identity checking and distance computation. Implements sqrt(JSD) as primary mechanism distance (critique resolution #1). Supports Gaussian closed-form (regression coefficients + residual variances) and nonparametric kernel-based estimation. Provides both pairwise and multi-distribution JSD computation.

Context-Aware DAG Alignment engine. Six-phase algorithm: anchor propagation, MB candidate generation, CI-fingerprint scoring, Hungarian matching, edge classification, score/divergence computation. Includes transitive consistency check across alignment triples.

Unified 4D plasticity descriptor computation. Components: structural plasticity (sqrt(JSD) over parent indicators), parametric plasticity (mean sqrt(JSD) of conditionals within parent-set groups), emergence (Markov blanket ratio), context sensitivity (CV of plasticity across context subsets). Classification with calibrated thresholds.

Curiosity-driven quality-diversity search over mechanism-change patterns. CVT tessellation via Lloyd's algorithm (critique resolution #7). MAP-Elites archive with elitist replacement. Curiosity signal combining inverse visit count novelty and quality prediction error surprise. Genome mutation operators for context/mechanism subset exploration.

Tipping-point detection via PELT on sqrt(JSD) mechanism divergence sequences (critique resolution #8). BIC-penalty changepoint detection with permutation validation. Mechanism-level attribution identifying which causal relationships undergo tipping-point transitions. Benjamini-Hochberg FDR correction for multiple changepoints.

Robustness certificate generation. Structural invariance via stability selection (subsample 50% x 100 rounds, critique resolution #2). Parametric stability via bootstrap UCB on max sqrt(JSD). Three certificate types: STRONG_INVARIANCE, PARAMETRIC_STABILITY, STRUCTURAL_STABILITY. Explicit validity conditions and robustness margins.

Structural stability analysis module. Implements SHD perturbation testing: injects controlled edge errors (s in {1,2,5,10}) into estimated DAGs and measures descriptor perturbation (critique resolution #5). Compares empirical perturbation against the theoretical bound f(s, n, d_max) from Theorem T8. Provides DAG estimation error propagation diagnostics.

Three-phase pipeline orchestrator. Phase 1 (Foundation): external causal discovery -> CADA alignment -> PDC descriptors. Phase 2 (Exploration): CD-QD search -> archive post-processing. Phase 3 (Validation): TPD tipping points -> RCG certificates -> atlas assembly. Manages parallelism (per-context discovery, per-pair alignment, per-variable descriptors, per-mechanism certificates). Produces the complete Causal-Plasticity Atlas output.

Synthetic and semi-synthetic benchmark generators. Generator 1 (FSVP): fixed structure, varying parameters. Generator 2 (CSAC): changing structure across contexts. Generator 3 (TPS): tipping-point scenarios. Generator 4 (LCE): latent confounder experiments (critique resolution #4). Generator 5 (NLB): nonlinear cubic spline SEMs (critique resolution #6). Semi-synthetic: Sachs, Alarm, Asia, Insurance networks with controlled modifications.
Do-calculus engine, counterfactual reasoning, interventional queries, and identifiability via the ID algorithm. 4 files, 3190 lines.
CPDAG and PAG representations, Meek orientation rules, d-separation via Bayes-Ball algorithm, and MEC enumeration. 5 files, 2555 lines.
Fisher-z partial correlation test, kernel CI (KCI/HSIC), discrete CI (chi-squared/G-test), conditional mutual information (KSG estimator), and adaptive test selection. 5 files, 2147 lines.
BIC and extended BIC (eBIC), BGe score (Bayesian Gaussian equivalent), BDeu score, interventional BIC, and decomposable score caching. 5 files, 1845 lines.
Order MCMC, partition MCMC, structure MCMC, parallel tempering across chains, and DAG bootstrap for posterior approximation. 5 files, 2430 lines.
Markov-blanket crossover, edge-preserving crossover, constrained mutation operators, and GES-style local search (insert/delete/turn). 4 files, 1839 lines.

All evaluation baselines: IND-PHC (independent per-context), pooled, ICP (invariant causal prediction), CD-NOD (causal discovery from nonstationary/heterogeneous data), JCI (joint causal inference), GES, and LSEM-pool. 7 files, 2596 lines.
MCMC convergence diagnostics, supermartingale-based stopping criteria, ergodicity checker, Sobol and Morris sensitivity analysis, and mechanism comparison utilities. 5 files, 1919 lines.

Synthetic benchmark generators SB1-SB5, nonlinear SCM generation (GP, sigmoid, quadratic mechanisms), semi-synthetic benchmarks (Sachs, ALARM, Insurance networks), and controlled perturbation injection. 4 files, 1723 lines.
Incremental Plasticity Update (IPU, Algorithm 6) for streaming contexts, online alignment maintenance, windowed CUSUM change detection, and stream buffer management. 4 files, 1703 lines.
Parent-set caching for score decomposition, approximate BIC for large graphs, sparse DAG storage and operations, and distributed evaluation utilities. 4 files, 1429 lines.
Experiment configuration and runner, hyperparameter spaces and tuning (grid, random, Bayesian), and component registry for modular pipeline assembly. 3 files, 976 lines.

Expanded certificate generation: Fisher information Lipschitz bounds, Boltzmann stability certificates, and Asymptotic Certificate Tightening (ACT, Algorithm 7). 3 new files added to existing certificates package.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On Generator 1 (fixed DAG, varying weights, p=20, K=10, n=1000), CPA correctly classifies >= 90% of edges into {invariant, plastic} as measured by macro-averaged F1.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Upper bound of 95% CI for macro-averaged F1 over 50 replications falls below 0.85.
falsifiable_claims.support: Lower bound of 95% CI exceeds 0.90. CPA outperforms IND-PHC baseline by >= 0.10 F1 points (paired Wilcoxon, p < 0.01).

falsifiable_claims.claim: On Generator 2 (changing DAG topology, p=15, K=8), CPA detects emergent edges with precision >= 0.85 and recall >= 0.80.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Upper 95% CI for precision below 0.80 OR upper 95% CI for recall below 0.75 across 50 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: Both precision and recall lower CI bounds exceed stated thresholds. Context boundary identification correct within +/-1 for >= 75% of emergent edges.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On Generator 3 (tipping-point scenarios, K=20), CPA localizes tipping points within +/-1 context index of truth for >= 80% of tipping-point edges.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Mean MAD over 50 replications exceeds 2.0 context indices, or 80th-percentile MAD exceeds 3.0.
falsifiable_claims.support: Mean MAD <= 1.0, 80th-percentile MAD <= 2.0, tipping-point detection AUC-ROC >= 0.90.

falsifiable_claims.claim: Robustness certificates are well-calibrated: among edges assigned confidence c, the fraction remaining in the same stability class under stability selection lies within [c-0.05, c+0.05] for each confidence bucket.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: For any confidence bucket, empirical coverage deviates from nominal by more than 0.10 on average over 30 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) <= 0.04 across all confidence buckets.

falsifiable_claims.claim: Curiosity-driven QD search fills >= 30% more CVT cells than uniform random search and >= 15% more than standard MAP-Elites, given 10,000 evaluations on Generator 2.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Coverage improvement over random search is less than 15% at upper 95% CI bound over 30 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: Improvement over random > 30% and over MAP-Elites > 15%, both with paired Wilcoxon p < 0.01 and Cohen's d >= 0.8.

falsifiable_claims.claim: CADA alignment achieves normalized alignment score >= 0.85 on Generator 2, outperforming naive node-identity alignment by >= 0.10 and VF2 by >= 0.05.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Upper 95% CI for alignment score below 0.80, or improvement over VF2 not significant at p < 0.05.
falsifiable_claims.support: Lower CI bound >= 0.85, improvements over both baselines significant with p < 0.01.

falsifiable_claims.claim: CPA completes full pipeline on p=50, K=10, n=500 in under 30 minutes wall-clock (single CPU core), and p=100, K=5, n=500 in under 120 minutes.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Median wall time over 10 replications exceeds 60 min for p=50 or 240 min for p=100.
falsifiable_claims.support: Median wall time <= stated limits. Time-vs-p curve fits polynomial of degree <= 3 (R^2 >= 0.95).

falsifiable_claims.claim: On semi-synthetic Sachs network (11 variables, 5 context variants), CPA recovers correct stability classification for >= 80% of 17 known edges.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Classification accuracy below 70% at upper 95% CI across 50 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: Accuracy >= 80%, precision for 'plastic' class >= 0.75, recall for 'plastic' class >= 0.75.

falsifiable_claims.claim: Information-theoretic plasticity descriptors achieve AUROC >= 0.90 for discriminating invariant from plastic edges on Generators 1-2 combined.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: AUROC below 0.85 at upper 95% CI via DeLong's test.
falsifiable_claims.support: AUROC >= 0.90 with DeLong 95% CI entirely above 0.88. Significantly more discriminative than variance-of-correlation baseline (paired DeLong, p < 0.01).

falsifiable_claims.claim: Given fixed random seeds, two independent CPA runs produce bit-identical stability class assignments and numerically identical descriptor values (to 10^-8 tolerance).
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Any pair of seeded runs produces differing class assignments or descriptor values differing by more than 10^-6.
falsifiable_claims.support: All 100 paired runs (10 benchmarks x 10 seed pairs) produce identical outputs within tolerance.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On Generator 4 (latent confounders, 5-10% hidden variables), CPA degrades gracefully: classification F1 decreases by no more than 0.15 compared to the no-confounder setting on the same DAG.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: F1 drop exceeds 0.25 at the lower 95% CI bound across 30 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: F1 drop <= 0.15, and the oracle baseline (true DAGs given) shows F1 drop <= 0.05, isolating the degradation to causal discovery errors.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On the SHD perturbation experiment (critique resolution #5), the observed plasticity descriptor perturbation is bounded by the theoretical f(s, n, d_max) from Theorem T8 for 95% of injected-error trials with s in {1, 2, 5, 10}.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: More than 10% of trials violate the bound for any s value across 50 replications.
falsifiable_claims.support: At least 95% of trials satisfy the bound for all s values. The empirical perturbation scales linearly with s as predicted.

falsifiable_claims.name: CPA vs CD-NOD
falsifiable_claims.criterion: On CSAC (p=15, K=8), CPA achieves strictly higher macro-averaged F1 than CD-NOD for binary changed/unchanged classification, with structural vs parametric decomposition unavailable to CD-NOD.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: CD-NOD F1 >= CPA F1 at upper 95% CI (paired Wilcoxon, p >= 0.05).
falsifiable_claims.support: CPA F1 exceeds CD-NOD F1 by >= 0.05 with p < 0.01 and Cohen's d >= 0.5.

falsifiable_claims.statement: On the original Sachs et al. (2005) multi-condition flow cytometry dataset (14 experimental conditions), CPA classifies >=70% of intervention-downstream edges as non-invariant and >=80% of non-downstream edges as invariant.
falsifiable_claims.falsification_condition: Upper 95% CI for downstream non-invariant rate < 0.70 OR upper 95% CI for non-downstream invariant rate < 0.80.
falsifiable_claims.support_condition: Lower 95% CI for downstream non-invariant rate > 0.70 AND lower 95% CI for non-downstream invariant rate > 0.80.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On FSVP with p=20, d_max=3, sigma=1.0, sweep n in {100,200,500,1000,2000,5000} with K=10, the empirical convergence slope of log(||psi_hat - psi*||_inf) vs log(n) lies in [-0.55, -0.45], consistent with the n^{-1/2} rate from Theorem T11.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Slope outside [-0.60, -0.40] at alpha=0.01 via linear regression t-test on R=50 replications x 6 sample sizes.
falsifiable_claims.support: Slope in [-0.52, -0.48] with 95% CI across R=50 replications.
falsifiable_claims.test: Linear regression of log-error on log-n; t-test for slope = -0.50.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On CSAC (p=15, K=8), pairwise mechanism distances d_mu satisfy: (a) triangle inequality for all triples (tolerance 10^-8); (b) Gaussian kernel Gram matrix has all eigenvalues >= -10^-8.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: (a) triangle inequality violated for >0.1% of triples, or (b) any Gram eigenvalue < -10^-6.
falsifiable_claims.support: Zero violations and minimum eigenvalue > 0 across R=50 replications.
falsifiable_claims.test: Exact enumeration of all triples (~12600 total); Bonferroni-corrected eigenvalue test.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On FSVP (p=20, K=10), the ratio eps_t*sqrt(t)/(d_max*sigma) converges to a constant C* in [0.5, 5.0] for per-context sample counts t in {50,100,200,500,1000,2000}.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: Ratio not in [0.1, 10.0] for t >= 500, or Spearman |rho| > 0.30 with p < 0.01.
falsifiable_claims.support: |rho| < 0.15 for t >= 200 and ratio in [0.5, 5.0] for t >= 500 across 90% of mechanisms.
falsifiable_claims.test: Spearman rank correlation of ratio vs t for tail (t >= 200).

falsifiable_claims.claim: On CSAC (p=15, K=8, Medium): (a) intrinsic dimensionality of descriptor point cloud <= 3; (b) sqrt(JSD) distances correlate with Fisher-Rao geodesic distances with r >= 0.95; (c) silhouette score of true plasticity classes >= 0.60.
falsifiable_claims.support: (a) dim in [2.0, 3.0], (b) r >= 0.95, (c) silhouette >= 0.60, all with 95% CIs.
falsifiable_claims.test: Bootstrap CIs on MLE intrinsic dim; Fisher z-transformed CI on Pearson r; bootstrap CI on silhouette.

falsifiable_claims.claim: On FSVP (Medium, p=20), ACT issues at least as many valid FWER-controlled certificates as uniform-budget RCG with the same B_total, while using fewer bootstrap samples on easy mechanisms.
falsifiable_claims.falsification: ACT issues fewer valid certificates than uniform-RCG (one-sided paired Wilcoxon, alpha=0.05, R=50).
falsifiable_claims.support: ACT issues >= 5% more certificates AND total bootstrap cost for uncertain mechanisms <= 80% of uniform allocation.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: Generator 1: Fixed Structure, Varying Parameters (FSVP)
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Test detection of parametric plasticity with constant DAG topology.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: Random Erdos-Renyi DAG, p in {10,20,50}, K in {5,10,20}, 60% invariant / 40% plastic edges. Invariant: constant weight w ~ Uniform([-1,-0.3] u [0.3,1]). Plastic: smoothly varying weight w(k) = b + r*(k-1)/(K-1). Linear Gaussian SEM with noise sigma in {0.5, 1.0, 2.0}.
synthetic_benchmarks.difficulty_levels: Easy (p=10, n=2000, sigma=0.5), Medium (p=20, n=1000, sigma=1.0), Hard (p=50, n=500, sigma=1.5).
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Per-edge labels {invariant, plastic}, weight matrices W^(k), full adjacency matrix A.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: Generator 2: Changing Structure Across Contexts (CSAC)
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Test detection of structural plasticity and emergence—edges appear, disappear, or reverse.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: Base DAG with p in {10,15,30,50}, K in {4,8,12}. 50% invariant, 25% plastic, 15% disappearing (at random context boundary), 10% emergent (new edges appearing), up to 2 edge reversals. Linear Gaussian SEM.
synthetic_benchmarks.difficulty_levels: Easy (p=10, K=4, no reversals), Medium (p=15, K=8, 1 reversal), Hard (p=30, K=8, 2 reversals).
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Per-context adjacency matrices, per-edge labels {invariant, plastic, emergent, disappearing, reversing}, transition context indices.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: Generator 3: Tipping-Point Scenarios (TPS)
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Test tipping-point detection—smooth drift followed by abrupt structural change.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: K in {15,20,30} ordered contexts. Select |T| in {2,5,10} edges for tipping-point transitions at theta* ~ Uniform([0.3,0.7]). Three tipping types: collapse (40%), bifurcation (30%), reversal (30%). Pre-tipping: linear drift. Post-tipping: discontinuous change. Includes 'noisy ordering' variant with permuted context indices.
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Per-edge tipping presence (binary), tipping location theta*, tipping type, pre/post structural configuration.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: Generator 4: Latent Confounder Experiments (LCE)
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Test system degradation under latent confounders, addressing critique resolution #4.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: Based on Generator 2 (CSAC) structure. Add 5-10% hidden variables (latent confounders) by generating a full DAG with p+h variables, then hiding h variables. The observed marginal DAGs contain spurious edges between children of latent confounders. Ground truth labels computed on the full (unconfounded) DAG. Evaluate CPA on the observed (confounded) DAGs.
synthetic_benchmarks.difficulty_levels: Mild (5% hidden, p=15), Moderate (10% hidden, p=20), Severe (15% hidden, p=30).
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Full DAG with all variables, observed DAG with confounders marginalized, per-edge plasticity labels on the full DAG, mapping of spurious edges to their latent causes.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: Generator 5: Nonlinear Benchmark (NLB)
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Test CPA on nonlinear SEMs using cubic spline mechanisms, addressing critique resolution #6.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: Based on Generator 1 (FSVP) structure but with nonlinear mechanisms: X_j = sum_{i in Pa(j)} g_ij(X_i) + epsilon_j where g_ij are cubic splines with randomly placed knots. Invariant mechanisms: same spline across contexts. Plastic mechanisms: spline coefficients vary smoothly. CPA must use a nonparametric CI test (kernel CI test) instead of partial correlation.
synthetic_benchmarks.difficulty_levels: Easy (p=10, n=2000, mild nonlinearity), Medium (p=20, n=1000, moderate nonlinearity), Hard (p=30, n=500, strong nonlinearity with 5 knots per spline).
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Same label structure as Generator 1 but with nonlinear ground-truth functions.

synthetic_benchmarks.name: SHD Perturbation Experiment
synthetic_benchmarks.purpose: Directly test Theorem T8 (structural stability) by injecting controlled DAG estimation errors, addressing critique resolution #5.
synthetic_benchmarks.specification: For each Generator 1/2 instance at Medium difficulty: take the true DAGs, inject s in {1, 2, 5, 10} random edge errors (additions, deletions, or reversals maintaining acyclicity). Run the full CPA pipeline on perturbed DAGs. Measure L-infinity distance between perturbed and true plasticity descriptors. Compare against the theoretical bound f(s, n, d_max).
synthetic_benchmarks.difficulty_levels: Applied to all generators at Medium difficulty. Error levels s in {1, 2, 5, 10}.
synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: True plasticity descriptors from unperturbed DAGs. Theoretical perturbation bound f(s, n, d_max) from Theorem T8.

semi_synthetic_benchmarks.name: Sachs Protein-Signaling Network
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.source: Sachs et al. (2005), 11 phospholipids/phosphoproteins, 17 known edges. Available via bnlearn::sachs.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.construction: Fit linear Gaussian SEM to expert-validated structure. Create K=5 contexts: baseline, pathway inhibition (3 edges perturbed), knockout (1 edge removed, 1 emergent), mild scaling (5 edges mildly plastic), bifurcation (1 reversal, 1 strength doubling). n in {500, 1000, 2000} per context.

semi_synthetic_benchmarks.name: Alarm Network
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.source: Beinlich et al. (1989), 37 variables, 46 edges. Medical diagnosis domain. Available via bnlearn::alarm.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.construction: Fit linear SEM. Create K=4 contexts simulating different patient populations: baseline, elderly (10 edges with shifted weights), pediatric (5 structural changes), critical care (tipping-point scenario with 3 bifurcations). n=1000 per context.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Per-edge stability labels per context pair.

semi_synthetic_benchmarks.name: Asia (Lung Cancer) Network
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.source: Lauritzen & Spiegelhalter (1988), 8 variables, 8 edges. Available via bnlearn::asia.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.construction: Gaussianized version with K=6 contexts representing different geographic regions. Invariant edges: tuberculosis->dyspnea. Plastic edges: smoking->cancer (varying strength). Emergent: pollution->cancer in urban contexts only. Small network enables exhaustive analysis.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Full per-edge classification across all 6 contexts.

semi_synthetic_benchmarks.name: Insurance Network
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.source: Binder et al. (1997), 27 variables, 52 edges. Available via bnlearn::insurance.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.construction: Create K=5 contexts representing different insurance markets. Modify risk factor relationships (10 edges with market-specific weights), regulatory regime changes (3 structural modifications), and emerging risk factors (2 new edges in 2 contexts). n=1000 per context.
semi_synthetic_benchmarks.ground_truth: Per-edge labels with market-specific modifications documented.

real_world_studies.name: GTEx Gene Expression
real_world_studies.description: Multi-tissue gene expression data from the GTEx consortium. Select 20-50 genes from a well-characterized pathway (e.g., p53 signaling). Contexts = tissues (e.g., brain, liver, heart, lung, muscle). Expect tissue-specific regulatory mechanisms (structural plasticity) alongside housekeeping gene invariance.
real_world_studies.expected_findings: Housekeeping genes classified as invariant. Tissue-specific transcription factors classified as structurally plastic or emergent. Known tissue-specific regulatory edges recovered.
real_world_studies.validation: Compare against curated tissue-specific regulatory databases (e.g., ENCODE, Roadmap Epigenomics).

real_world_studies.name: Penn World Table Economic Indicators
real_world_studies.description: Cross-country macroeconomic panel data. Select 15-25 economic indicators (GDP, trade, investment, education, etc.). Contexts = countries or country groups. Expect invariant macroeconomic relationships (Okun's law) alongside country-specific institutional effects.
real_world_studies.expected_findings: Core macroeconomic relationships (investment-growth) classified as invariant. Trade-institution relationships classified as plastic. Development-stage transitions detected as tipping points.
real_world_studies.validation: Compare against known stylized facts in development economics. Use block bootstrap for time-series dependence (critique resolution from red team).

real_world_studies.name: Sachs Protein-Signalling Network (Original Multi-Condition)
real_world_studies.description: Original Sachs et al. (2005) flow cytometry dataset with 7,466 cells across 14 experimental conditions. Contexts = experimental conditions with known intervention targets. Validates CPA plasticity labels against known intervention-downstream edges.
real_world_studies.ground_truth: Known intervention targets from original study; edges downstream of interventions should be classified as non-invariant.

baselines.name: IND-PHC (Independent Per-Context + Post-Hoc Comparison)
baselines.description: Run PC or GES independently on each context. Compare DAGs post-hoc using raw SHD and Jaccard index over edges. Classify edges as invariant if present in all contexts with same orientation, plastic otherwise. No formal certificates or tipping-point detection.
baselines.purpose: Naive baseline. Measures how much CPA improves over the simplest multi-context approach.

baselines.name: ICP (Invariant Causal Prediction)
baselines.description: Peters et al. (2016). Identifies invariant causal predictors across environments. Provides binary invariant/non-invariant classification with confidence guarantees. Does not detect emergence, structural plasticity, or tipping points. Applied per-target variable across all K environments.
baselines.purpose: Principled baseline with formal guarantees. Tests whether CPA's richer plasticity spectrum adds value beyond ICP's binary classification.

baselines.name: Pooled-PC (Pooled Data Single Discovery)
baselines.description: Pool all data across contexts into a single dataset. Run PC algorithm once. All edges are implicitly 'invariant' (single DAG for all contexts). Cannot detect any form of plasticity.
baselines.purpose: Tests whether multi-context analysis provides value over single-context analysis on pooled data.

baselines.name: JCI (Joint Causal Inference)
baselines.description: Mooij et al. (2020). Joint causal inference across multiple datasets with different experimental conditions. Infers a single DAG with context-dependent edge annotations. Handles interventional data but does not produce plasticity descriptors or certificates.

baselines.name: PELT-on-Correlations
baselines.description: Compute pairwise correlations for each variable pair across ordered contexts. Apply PELT changepoint detection directly to the correlation sequence. A reviewer-suggested simple alternative to CPA's mechanism-divergence-based tipping-point detection.
baselines.purpose: Tests whether the causal-structure-aware tipping-point detection (ALG4) outperforms a simpler correlation-based approach.

baselines.name: Oracle-DAG + CPA-Classifier
baselines.description: Provide the TRUE ground-truth DAGs to the CPA pipeline (bypassing causal discovery). Run only the plasticity classification pipeline (ALG2-5). This isolates the classification contribution from causal discovery errors (critique resolution #3).
baselines.purpose: Oracle baseline measuring the ceiling performance of the plasticity classification module, independent of DAG estimation quality. Essential for disentangling classification errors from discovery errors.

baselines.name: CD-NOD (Causal Discovery from Nonstationary/heterogeneous Data)
baselines.description: Huang et al. (2020). Causal discovery method designed for heterogeneous data with changing causal modules. Detects which modules change across domains. Does not provide plasticity spectrum or certificates.
baselines.purpose: Specialized baseline for heterogeneous causal discovery. Tests whether CPA's post-hoc analysis matches an integrated discovery-and-change-detection approach.

baselines.name: BOCPD (Bayesian Online Changepoint Detection)
baselines.description: Adams & MacKay (2007). Bayesian approach to online changepoint detection on mechanism divergence sequences. Alternative to the frequentist PELT approach used in ALG4.
baselines.purpose: Bayesian alternative for tipping-point detection, testing whether CPA's PELT + permutation approach outperforms a fully Bayesian method.

baselines.name: Random QD Search
baselines.description: Replace curiosity-driven CD-QD with uniform random sampling of genome space. Same evaluation budget (10,000 evaluations).

baselines.name: Standard MAP-Elites
baselines.description: MAP-Elites with isoline variation and random mutation but without curiosity signal (mu=0). Tests whether curiosity-driven exploration adds value.

baselines.name: CD-NOD (Huang et al. 2020)
baselines.description: Multi-environment causal discovery exploiting distributional changes via domain-index variable C and kernel-based conditional independence tests (KCI). Produces per-variable binary labels (changed/unchanged). Maps to CPA: unchanged->invariant, changed->non-invariant (collapsed). Provides no tipping-point localisation or certificates.
baselines.purpose: Tests whether CPA's finer 4-class decomposition provides value beyond binary change detection. CD-NOD is the only baseline that jointly reasons about multiple contexts.

baselines.name: NOTEARS+PHC (Zheng et al. 2018)
baselines.description: Continuous optimization structure learning with acyclicity constraint h(W)=tr(exp(W*W))-p=0, run independently per context, thresholded at |w_ij|>0.3, then post-hoc comparison. Tests whether continuous optimization improves per-context DAG quality.
baselines.purpose: Tests whether CPA's advantage over per-context methods is robust to the per-context estimator quality (continuous vs constraint-based).

baselines.name: GOLEM+PHC (Ng et al. 2020)
baselines.description: Likelihood-based continuous DAG learning with soft penalty, GOLEM-NV variant for heteroscedastic noise, run independently per context with post-hoc comparison.
baselines.purpose: Tests robustness of CPA advantage to heteroscedastic noise handling by per-context estimators.

baselines.name: DAGMA+PHC (Bello et al. 2022)
baselines.description: M-matrix characterization of DAGs (sI - W*W positive definite) for smoother constraint landscape, run independently per context with post-hoc comparison.
baselines.purpose: Tests whether CPA's multi-context joint inference adds value beyond improved per-context estimates (DAGMA has smoother optimization landscape).

metrics.name: Macro-Averaged F1 Score
metrics.definition: Harmonic mean of precision and recall, averaged across all plasticity classes (invariant, parametric_plastic, structural_plastic, emergent). Treats each class equally regardless of prevalence.

metrics.name: Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD) of Tipping-Point Localization
metrics.definition: For each true tipping point tau*, compute |hat{tau} - tau*| where hat{tau} is the nearest detected tipping point. Average over all tipping-point edges and replications.

metrics.name: Expected Calibration Error (ECE)
metrics.definition: For certificates with nominal confidence c, compute ECE = sum_b (|B_b|/N) * |empirical_coverage_b - nominal_b| over confidence buckets B = {0.7, 0.8, 0.9, 0.95}.

metrics.name: Archive Coverage Ratio
metrics.definition: Fraction of reachable CVT cells occupied after a fixed number of evaluations. Reachable cells estimated by running a very long (100K evaluation) random search.

metrics.name: Normalized Alignment Score
metrics.definition: 1 - (normalized SHD between aligned DAGs and ground-truth correspondence) / max_possible_SHD. Ranges from 0 (worst) to 1 (perfect alignment).

metrics.name: Wall-Clock Time (seconds)
metrics.definition: Total elapsed time for the full pipeline from raw data to atlas output, measured on a single CPU core (Intel i7 equivalent, >= 3.0 GHz, 32 GB RAM).

metrics.name: AUROC for Descriptor Discriminability
metrics.definition: Area under the ROC curve when using the plasticity descriptor magnitude (L2 norm of psi) as a binary classifier score for invariant vs. non-invariant edges. Assessed via DeLong's test with 95% CI.

metrics.name: Tipping-Point Detection AUC-ROC
metrics.definition: Binary classification AUC-ROC: for each context index, is there a true tipping point? Detector output is the maximum mechanism divergence at that index. Aggregated over all edges and replications.

metrics.name: Structural Hamming Distance (SHD)
metrics.definition: Number of edge additions, deletions, and reversals needed to transform one DAG into another. Used as the error metric in the SHD perturbation experiment (FC12).

metrics.name: Descriptor Perturbation (L-infinity)
metrics.definition: L-infinity norm of the difference between plasticity descriptors computed from true vs. perturbed DAGs: ||hat{psi} - psi||_inf = max(|hat{psi}_S - psi_S|, |hat{psi}_P - psi_P|, |hat{psi}_E - psi_E|, |hat{psi}_CS - psi_CS|). Used to validate Theorem T8.

metrics.name: Cohen's d Effect Size
metrics.definition: Standardized mean difference between CPA and baseline performance: d = (mean_CPA - mean_baseline) / pooled_std. Used to quantify practical significance beyond statistical significance.

metrics.name: Certificate Issuance Rate
metrics.definition: Fraction of truly invariant mechanisms for which a certificate is successfully issued (true positive rate of certificate generation). Measures the system's willingness to commit, not just its accuracy.

ablations.name: Curiosity Signal Ablation
ablations.description: Run QD search with curiosity weight mu=0 (no surprise signal) vs. mu=0.5 (default). Measure archive coverage, quality distribution, and convergence speed. Tests whether curiosity-driven exploration adds value over pure novelty-based MAP-Elites.
ablations.expected_outcome: Curiosity improves coverage by >= 10% and convergence speed by >= 20%.

ablations.name: DAG Alignment Ablation
ablations.description: Replace CADA (ALG1) with naive node-identity alignment (assume variables with same index correspond). Run full pipeline and measure classification F1 degradation. Tests the value of the alignment step.
ablations.expected_outcome: F1 drops by >= 0.10 without proper alignment on Generator 2 (where variable correspondence is non-trivial).

ablations.name: Certificate Module Ablation
ablations.description: Remove ALG5 (RCG) and report classifications without confidence guarantees. Measure the fraction of classifications that would be overturned under bootstrap resampling. Tests whether certificates identify unreliable classifications.
ablations.expected_outcome: Without certificates, >= 15% of 'invariant' classifications are overturned under bootstrap, demonstrating the value of formal guarantees.

ablations.name: Descriptor Component Ablation
ablations.description: Remove each plasticity descriptor component individually (psi_S, psi_P, psi_E, psi_CS) and measure classification F1 and AUROC. Tests which components are essential for discrimination.
ablations.expected_outcome: Removing psi_S degrades structural change detection by >= 0.20 F1. Removing psi_P degrades parametric change detection by >= 0.15 F1. psi_E is essential for emergence (Generator 2). psi_CS has the smallest marginal contribution.

ablations.name: Tipping-Point Detection Ablation
ablations.description: Replace PELT-based tipping-point detection (ALG4) with a simple sliding-window approach (compare average divergence in windows of size 3 before and after each context index, using a t-test). Tests whether the sophisticated PELT approach outperforms a simple baseline.
ablations.expected_outcome: PELT achieves >= 0.10 better AUC-ROC than sliding window, especially for multiple tipping points (where PELT's segmentation approach is superior).

ablations.name: Mechanism Distance Ablation
ablations.description: Replace sqrt(JSD) mechanism distance with raw Euclidean distance on regression coefficients (no information-theoretic grounding). Measure classification F1 and tipping-point detection accuracy. Tests the value of the information-theoretic foundation.
ablations.expected_outcome: sqrt(JSD) outperforms Euclidean by >= 0.05 F1 on classification and >= 0.05 AUC-ROC on tipping-point detection, due to scale-invariance and proper metric properties.

ablations.name: Stability Selection vs. Full Bootstrap
ablations.description: Compare stability selection (50% subsample x 100 rounds) against full bootstrap (500 DAG re-estimations per pair) for structural certificate generation. Measure certificate calibration (ECE), issuance rate, and wall-clock time. Tests critique resolution #2.
ablations.expected_outcome: Stability selection achieves comparable ECE (within 0.02) with >= 10x speedup. Full bootstrap is more conservative (lower issuance rate) but not significantly better calibrated.

builds_on.reference: Peters et al. (2016) — Invariant Causal Prediction (ICP)
builds_on.relationship: CPA extends ICP's binary invariant/non-invariant classification to a continuous plasticity spectrum with four dimensions. ICP identifies which predictors have invariant causal effects across environments; CPA classifies the full mechanism-change pattern (structural, parametric, emergent) and provides robustness certificates.
