CPA uses stability selection as the core technique for structural invariance certificates (ALG5, Step 1), replacing expensive full bootstrap DAG re-estimation. The Shah-Bühlmann (2013) bound provides the error rate control for certificate soundness (Theorem T3).

builds_on.reference: Killick et al. (2012) — PELT (Pruned Exact Linear Time)
builds_on.relationship: CPA adapts PELT for tipping-point detection on mechanism divergence sequences (ALG4). The core dynamic programming and pruning structure are borrowed; the cost function is novel (within-segment mechanism homogeneity rather than Gaussian likelihood).

CPA adapts MAP-Elites for exploring the space of mechanism-change patterns (ALG3). The archive structure and elitist replacement are standard; the behavior descriptor (plasticity descriptor) and quality function (classification entropy + significance) are domain-specific contributions.

builds_on.reference: Endres & Schindelin (2003) — sqrt(JSD) as a metric
builds_on.relationship: CPA uses sqrt(JSD) as the primary mechanism distance (critique resolution #1), leveraging the proof that sqrt(JSD) is a proper metric satisfying the triangle inequality. This ensures Theorem T1 holds and the CVT tessellation has geometric validity.

builds_on.reference: Spirtes, Glymour & Scheines (2000) — Causation, Prediction, and Search
builds_on.relationship: CPA assumes causal DAGs as input, estimated by constraint-based (PC) or score-based (GES) methods from this foundational work. The faithfulness assumption (A2) and causal Markov condition are inherited from this framework.

builds_on.reference: Friedman et al. (1999) — Bootstrap DAG Learning
builds_on.relationship: The parametric bootstrap in ALG5 (Step 3) for constructing confidence intervals on Fisher-Rao distances is adapted from Friedman et al.'s approach to assessing DAG feature confidence. CPA extends this from single-context DAG confidence to multi-context mechanism invariance certificates.

builds_on.reference: Bareinboim & Pearl (2014) — Transportability from Multiple Environments
builds_on.relationship: CPA's invariant/plastic classification can be viewed as an empirical approximation of the transportability calculus. Invariant mechanisms correspond to transportable causal effects; plastic mechanisms require adjustment formulas for cross-context generalization. CPA provides a data-driven approach to what transportability theory addresses via do-calculus.

extends.reference: Huang et al. (2020) — CD-NOD (Causal Discovery from Nonstationary/heterogeneous Data)
extends.how_extended: CD-NOD detects which causal modules change across domains but does not classify the type of change (structural vs. parametric vs. emergent) or provide robustness certificates. CPA extends CD-NOD's change detection with a formal plasticity spectrum, certificate framework, and QD-based exploration of mechanism-change patterns.

extends.reference: Mooij et al. (2020) — JCI (Joint Causal Inference)
extends.how_extended: JCI infers a single DAG with context-dependent edge annotations but does not provide plasticity descriptors, tipping-point detection, or robustness certificates. CPA extends JCI by treating multi-context causal analysis as a post-discovery analysis problem with richer output (atlas vs. single annotated DAG).

extends.reference: Gao et al. (2010) — Graph Edit Distance (GED)
extends.how_extended: CPA's DAG alignment cost (Definition 6, Theorem T1) extends standard GED with: (i) mechanism-distance-weighted node substitution costs (using sqrt(JSD) between conditional distributions), (ii) explicit handling of variable-set mismatch (V_a != V_b), and (iii) causal-structure-aware candidate pruning via Markov blanket overlap.

CPA's curiosity signal (ALG3) extends novelty search with a quality component (MAP-Elites) and a surprise component (quality prediction error). The domain-specific adaptation to causal mechanism space with CVT tessellation is new.

differs_from.reference: Tian & Pearl (2001) — Causal Discovery from Changes
differs_from.key_difference: Tian & Pearl use differences between experimental and observational distributions to identify causal effects. CPA operates in a purely observational multi-context setting and classifies mechanism-change patterns rather than identifying causal effects. CPA does not require interventional data.

Anchor regression provides worst-case prediction guarantees under distribution shifts but does not characterize the type or location of mechanism changes. CPA provides a fine-grained plasticity classification rather than a minimax prediction guarantee.

differs_from.reference: Zhang et al. (2017) — Causal Discovery from Multiple Datasets with Non-identical Variable Sets
differs_from.key_difference: Zhang et al. focus on integrating information across datasets with different variable sets to improve a single causal graph. CPA preserves per-context DAGs and characterizes how they differ, rather than merging them into a consensus structure.

differs_from.reference: Heinze-Deml et al. (2018) — Invariant Causal Mechanisms
differs_from.key_difference: ICM assumes that invariant mechanisms are the norm and non-invariant ones are exceptions. CPA treats the full spectrum of invariance-to-plasticity as the primary object of study, with no prior assumption about the prevalence of either. The QD archive explicitly seeks to discover all patterns, not just invariant ones.

differs_from.reference: Pfister et al. (2019) — Invariance-Based Causal Structure Learning
differs_from.key_difference: Pfister et al. use invariance across environments as a signal for causal discovery. CPA takes estimated causal structures as input and analyzes their variation, operating downstream of causal discovery rather than using invariance for discovery.

technical_risks.risk: DAG estimation errors dominate plasticity classification errors
technical_risks.description: The entire CPA pipeline assumes causal DAGs estimated from observational data. With p=50 variables and n=500 samples, causal discovery algorithms (PC, GES) routinely produce DAGs with SHD >= 10. Theorem T8 bounds descriptor perturbation but the bound may be loose for large s. If DAG errors are systematic (e.g., consistently missing weak edges), the perturbation bound may underestimate the true descriptor error.
technical_risks.likelihood: HIGH — this is the most common source of error in practice.
technical_risks.impact: Certificates may be invalid. Plasticity classifications may be wrong. The entire atlas could be misleading.

technical_risks.risk: Faithfulness violations cause catastrophic parent set misidentification
technical_risks.description: Near-faithfulness violations (path coefficients that nearly cancel) cause constraint-based methods to miss or add edges. This is not covered by the SHD perturbation bound because the errors are systematic, not random. Uhler et al. (2013) showed the set of unfaithful distributions has positive Lebesgue measure in Gaussian models.
technical_risks.likelihood: MODERATE — depends on the domain. Biological networks with feedback-like motifs are particularly susceptible.
technical_risks.impact: Structural plasticity descriptor (psi_S) becomes unreliable. Mechanisms may be misclassified as structurally plastic when they are merely near-unfaithful.

technical_risks.risk: sqrt(JSD) estimation in high-dimensional parent sets has large variance
technical_risks.description: For parent sets of size d_max=5, estimating conditional distributions requires 4-dimensional density estimation. With n=500 samples, the convergence rate is O(n^{-2/(d+4)}) = O(n^{-2/9}) ~ 0.27 relative error. The parametric (Gaussian) assumption mitigates this for linear SEMs but not for nonlinear benchmarks.
technical_risks.likelihood: MODERATE — mitigated by Gaussian assumption but relevant for Generator 5 (nonlinear).
technical_risks.impact: Parametric plasticity descriptor (psi_P) has wide confidence intervals, reducing the power to distinguish invariant from mildly plastic mechanisms.

technical_risks.risk: CVT tessellation quality degrades in non-uniform descriptor distributions
technical_risks.description: Lloyd's algorithm assumes the plasticity descriptor distribution is reasonably smooth. If descriptors cluster in a small region of R^4 (which may happen when most mechanisms are invariant), many CVT cells will be empty by construction, and the coverage metric becomes meaningless.
technical_risks.likelihood: MODERATE — real-world datasets may have highly skewed plasticity distributions.
technical_risks.impact: QD archive coverage metrics are misleading. The 30% improvement claim (FC5) may be an artifact of tessellation rather than genuine exploration.

assumption_risks.description: ICM (assumption A3) states that knowing P(Pa_i) gives no information about P(X_i|Pa_i). If ICM fails (common in systems with shared latent causes), the plasticity descriptor conflates marginal distribution shifts with genuine mechanism changes. An edge may appear 'parametrically plastic' simply because the distribution of its parents changes across contexts, not because the mechanism itself changes.
assumption_risks.likelihood: MODERATE — ICM is empirically common in engineered systems but questionable in biological/social systems.
assumption_risks.impact: Plasticity classifications are unreliable. Certificates based on parametric stability may be meaningless.

assumption_risks.risk: Causal sufficiency (no unmeasured confounders) is violated
assumption_risks.description: Generator 4 (LCE) tests this explicitly (critique resolution #4). With latent confounders, the observed DAG contains spurious edges between children of hidden variables. These spurious edges appear as structural differences across contexts (when different confounders are active in different contexts), leading to false 'structural plasticity' or 'emergence' classifications.
assumption_risks.likelihood: HIGH — unmeasured confounders are the norm in observational studies.
assumption_risks.impact: False emergence and false structural plasticity detections. Certificates explicitly state the causal sufficiency assumption but cannot detect its violation.

assumption_risks.description: Tipping-point detection (Theorem T5) assumes piecewise Lipschitz context dependence with finitely many discontinuities. Chaotic or fractal-like context dependence violates this. The synthetic generators trivially satisfy B3 by construction, so the evaluation does not probe this boundary.
assumption_risks.likelihood: LOW for physical/biological systems, MODERATE for social/economic systems.
assumption_risks.impact: Tipping-point detection produces spurious or missed changepoints. The consistency guarantee (Theorem T5) becomes vacuous.

assumption_risks.risk: I.I.D. sampling within contexts fails for real-world data
assumption_risks.description: Real-world datasets (GTEx, PWT) may have temporal or spatial dependence within contexts. The bootstrap confidence intervals and sample complexity guarantees (Theorems T3, T4) assume i.i.d. sampling.
assumption_risks.likelihood: HIGH for time-series economic data, MODERATE for cross-sectional biological data.
assumption_risks.impact: Confidence intervals are too narrow (anti-conservative), certificates are overconfident. Mitigation: use block bootstrap for time-series, cluster bootstrap for spatial data.

scalability_risks.risk: Certificate generation exceeds time budget for large n
scalability_risks.description: Even with stability selection (100 rounds instead of 500*K^2 re-estimations), certificate generation for n=100 invariant mechanisms with K=10 contexts requires ~60,000 DAG re-estimations. With T_DAG ~ 1 second for GES on n=100: ~17 hours sequential. This may exceed the 120-minute budget for the p=100 case (FC7).
scalability_risks.likelihood: HIGH — this is the binding computational constraint.
scalability_risks.impact: FC7 may be falsified for p=100. Pipeline may need to selectively certify only the most important mechanisms rather than all invariant ones.

scalability_risks.risk: Pairwise alignment cache grows quadratically in K
scalability_risks.description: For K=50 contexts, the alignment cache contains K*(K-1)/2 = 1225 alignment results. Each stores O(n^2) data. For n=100: ~1225 * 10^4 = ~12M entries. Memory: ~100 MB. Compute: 1225 * O(n^2 * d^2) ~ 3 * 10^8 operations.
scalability_risks.likelihood: MODERATE — manageable for K <= 50 but becomes problematic for K > 100.
scalability_risks.impact: Memory and compute constraints for large K. Mitigation: hierarchical context clustering for K > 100.

scalability_risks.risk: QD search stagnates for high-dimensional mechanism spaces
scalability_risks.description: For n=100 variables, the genome space has 2^100 possible mechanism subsets. The mutation operator explores this space one element at a time, potentially requiring O(n) mutations to reach a distant target. With max_iter=5000 and pop_size=100, only 500K genomes are evaluated.
scalability_risks.likelihood: LOW — the CVT tessellation with L=1000 cells is small enough for practical coverage.
scalability_risks.impact: Archive may miss rare mechanism-change patterns that require large mechanism subsets. Mitigated by the curiosity signal driving exploration toward empty cells.

mitigations.mitigation: SHD Perturbation Experiment (critique resolution #5): Run SB6 to empirically validate Theorem T8's bound and characterize the actual descriptor perturbation under realistic DAG errors. If the bound is too loose, calibrate it empirically. Additionally, run the Oracle-DAG baseline (BL6, critique resolution #3) to isolate classification performance from discovery errors.
mitigations.effort: LOW — requires only generating perturbed DAGs and re-running the pipeline.

mitigations.mitigation: Latent Confounder Experiment (Generator 4, critique resolution #4): Explicitly test system degradation with 5-15% hidden variables. If performance collapses, add a MAG/PAG pathway that represents latent confounders explicitly, at the cost of weaker identifiability. Report degradation curves as a function of confounder fraction.
mitigations.effort: MODERATE — requires implementing Generator 4 and potentially adding MAG support.

mitigations.mitigation: Stability selection (critique resolution #2) reduces certificate computation from O(K^2 * B_s * T_DAG) per mechanism to O(K * 100 * T_DAG) per mechanism — a ~5K/100 = 50x reduction for K=10. For the p=100 case, selectively certify only mechanisms with borderline classifications (where the descriptor is near a threshold boundary), reducing n_inv to ~20% of invariant mechanisms.
mitigations.effort: LOW — stability selection is already implemented in the revised ALG5.

mitigations.mitigation: For nonlinear benchmarks (Generator 5, critique resolution #6), replace partial correlation CI tests with kernel-based CI tests (KCIT or RCIT). Replace the Gaussian Fisher-Rao closed-form with kernel-based MMD for mechanism distance. This increases computational cost by ~10x but provides valid inference for nonlinear mechanisms.
mitigations.effort: MODERATE — requires implementing nonparametric alternatives and validating on Generator 5.

mitigations.mitigation: Add a diagnostic test for ICM violations: compute the mutual information I(P(Pa_i); P(X_i|Pa_i)) across contexts. If this exceeds a threshold (calibrated by permutation), flag the mechanism as potentially violating ICM and widen the confidence intervals accordingly. This does not solve the problem but makes the system honest about it.
mitigations.effort: LOW — mutual information estimation is a byproduct of the existing descriptor computation.

mitigations.mitigation: For time-series real-world data (PWT), use block bootstrap with block size 5 years instead of standard bootstrap. For cross-sectional data with spatial dependence, use cluster bootstrap with clusters defined by geographic proximity. Document the modified bootstrap in the methods section.
mitigations.effort: LOW — requires swapping the bootstrap subroutine in ALG5.

mitigations.mitigation: Adaptive CVT tessellation: after the initial CVT construction, periodically re-run Lloyd's algorithm on the accumulated descriptor samples (including those from the QD search). This adapts the tessellation to the actual descriptor distribution, placing more cells in high-density regions and fewer in empty regions. Re-tessellate every 1000 iterations.
mitigations.effort: LOW — Lloyd's algorithm runs in seconds on 1000 cells with 4D descriptors.

mitigations.mitigation: Causal discovery algorithm sensitivity experiment: run the full CPA pipeline with two different discovery algorithms (PC and GES) on the same data. Measure classification agreement (Cohen's kappa). If agreement is high (kappa > 0.8), the results are robust to the discovery method. If agreement is low, flag the mechanisms with disagreements as requiring more data or interventional confirmation.
mitigations.effort: MODERATE — requires running the pipeline twice with different discovery algorithms.

lemmas.name: sqrt(JSD) Satisfies Triangle Inequality
lemmas.statement: The square root of the Jensen-Shannon divergence, d_mu(P,Q) = sqrt(JSD(P||Q)), is a proper metric satisfying non-negativity, symmetry, identity of indiscernibles, and the triangle inequality.

lemmas.name: Alignment Composition (Variable Correspondence Composition)
lemmas.statement: For partial injections alpha: V_a → V_b and beta: V_b → V_c, the composite gamma = beta ∘ alpha is a valid partial injection with |Dom(gamma)| >= |Dom(alpha) ∩ beta^{-1}(V_c)|.

lemmas.name: Cost Sub-Additivity
lemmas.statement: Under composite alignment gamma = beta ∘ alpha, each cost component satisfies C_k(gamma) <= C_k(alpha) + C_k(beta) for k in {structural, mechanism, variable-set}.

lemmas.name: MB Pruning Soundness
lemmas.statement: Under faithfulness, if pi* is the optimal alignment and (u_a, pi*(u_a)) is an optimal pair, the Jaccard overlap of their anchored Markov blankets satisfies J(u_a, pi*(u_a)) >= rho for a constant rho > 0.

lemmas.name: Lipschitz Property of Gaussian JSD
lemmas.statement: For Gaussian conditionals with residual variance >= sigma_min^2 > 0, the JSD is Lipschitz in (beta, sigma^2) with constant L_JSD = O(d_max/sigma) on the domain of bounded coefficients.

lemmas.name: Bootstrap CLT for Smooth Functionals
lemmas.statement: For smooth functionals of the empirical distribution, the bootstrap consistently estimates the sampling distribution, ensuring super-uniformity of p-values under the null.
